Krithivasan**, needs: - **High Conceptual Skill** — to envision TCS's role in the global AI revolution, decide which markets to enter, and plan $30+ billion revenue growth. - **High Human Skill** — to inspire 600,000+ employees, speak at global forums, and represent India's IT industry. - **Low Technical Skill** — the CEO need not write code; he relies on the CIOs and CTOs for technical expertise. **Conclusion:** The example confirms Katz's principle — **technical skill dominance decreases** and **conceptual skill dominance increases** as one climbs the corporate ladder, while **human skill remains a constant** at all levels. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks on This Topic:** > > 1. **Mixing up Roles with Skills:** Students often confuse Mintzberg's roles with Katz's skills. *Roles* = what a manager DOES; *Skills* = what a manager IS GOOD AT. Examiner deducts **1-2 marks** for this confusion. > > 2. **Forgetting Examples:** Definitions alone fetch 50% marks. **Real-world examples** (Tata, Infosys, Apple, Reliance) get you the rest. **Always include at least one example per role/skill.** > > 3. **No Diagram / No Table:** For a 14-mark question, omitting a table or diagram is a guaranteed **2-mark penalty**. Always include at least one structured visual. > > 4. **Writing the Acronym Wrong:** It is **FILM — EDDRNN** (or similar), NOT random listings. Examiners recognise the standard structure. > > 5. **Ignoring Katz's Hierarchy Inversion:** Many students state the three skills but **forget to discuss how the mix changes by level**. This is the **most-tested** aspect. Do not skip it. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] **Manager** = gets work done *through* other people. - [x] **Mintzberg (1973)** = 10 Roles in 3 Categories. - [x] **Katz (1955)** = 3 Skills (Technical, Human, Conceptual). - [x] **Interpersonal Roles** = Figurehead, Leader, Liaison (arise from formal authority). - [x] **Informational Roles** = Monitor, Disseminator, Spokesperson (arise from access to info). - [x] **Decisional Roles** = Entrepreneur, Disturbance Handler, Resource Allocator, Negotiator (arise from decision-making power). - [x] **First-Line Manager** = Technical-heavy, Conceptual-light. - [x] **Top Manager** = Conceptual-heavy, Technical-light. - [x] **Human Skill** = **Constant across all levels** (this is the *only* constant). - [x] **Fayol's Functions** (Plan, Organise, Command, Control) are related but **different** from Mintzberg's Roles. ### 🧠 High-Yield One-Liners for Last-Minute Revision 1. *"Mintzberg describes the **chess pieces**; Katz teaches us **how to play**."* 2. *"The Leader role is the only Interpersonal role with **direct** team impact."* 3. *"Resource Allocator is the **most powerful** Decisional role."* 4. *"Katz's model is **prescriptive** (what should be); Mintzberg's is **descriptive** (what is)."* 5. *"In startups, even **Top Managers** need high Technical Skill — Katz's rule has exceptions."* ### 📚 KTU Module 1 — Key Theorists You Must Remember | Theorist | Year | Contribution | KTU Exam Importance | |---|---|---|---| | Henri Fayol | 1916 | 14 Principles, 5 Functions | High | | Henry Mintzberg | 1973 | 10 Managerial Roles | **Very High** | | Robert Katz | 1955 | 3 Managerial Skills | **Very High** | | Fred Luthans | 1988 | Real Managers (4 activities) | Moderate | | Chester Barnard | 1938 | Acceptance Theory of Authority | Moderate | > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions on this topic, use the structure: > 1. **Definition** (1-2 lines) → 1 mark > 2. **Framework / Table** → 4-5 marks > 3. **Detailed Explanation with Examples** → 5-6 marks > 4. **Critical Analysis or Application** → 2-3 marks > > This template consistently scores **12-14 / 14** in KTU evaluations. --- *End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Manager's Roles and Skills* *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | PESFT418 | Organisational Behaviour*

topics.title: Behaviour at Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT418 - ORGANISATIONAL BEHAVIOUR
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Behaviour at Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Behaviour at Work** is the systematic study and analysis of the actions, conduct, and performance of individuals, groups, and structures within an organizational context. It is the foundational construct of Organisational Behaviour (OB) that examines how human behaviour influences, and is influenced by, the workplace environment. According to **Stephen P. Robbins** (a standard reference for KTU OB modules), behaviour at work is the field of study that investigates the impact that individuals, groups, and structure have on behaviour within organizations, for the purpose of applying such knowledge toward improving an organization's effectiveness.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Statement (For 2/3 Mark Questions):**
> "Behaviour at Work is the study of human behaviour in organizational settings, the interface between human behaviour and the organization, and the organization itself. It examines three key determinants: *individual, group, and organizational* level factors."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Theatre of Work" Model

Imagine a **stage play** (the organization). The **script** represents the organizational policies and processes. The **actors** are the employees. The **audience** represents the customers and stakeholders. The **director** is the management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Intuition:** Just as a play fails if the actors do not understand their roles, the script is unclear, or the director is autocratic, an organization fails when individual behaviour (poor attitudes), group behaviour (toxic teams), and organizational structure (rigid hierarchy) are misaligned. **Behaviour at Work is the "dramaturgy"** that aligns all three elements for a successful performance.

### 1.3 Three Levels of Behaviour at Work (KTU Core Framework)

| Level | Focus | Key Construct | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Individual Level** | Personality, Perception, Motivation, Attitudes | Psychological & biographical factors | A shy employee avoiding teamwork |
| **Group Level** | Norms, Roles, Team Dynamics, Communication | Socio-psychological factors | Groupthink in a project team |
| **Organizational Level** | Structure, Culture, Leadership, Power | Sociological factors | Hierarchical vs flat organizations |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember:** **"I-G-O"** = **I**ndividual, **G**roup, **O**rganization. KTU questions often ask students to "explain the three levels of analysis" — memorize this trifecta.

### 1.4 Why Study Behaviour at Work? (KTU Expected Answer Points)

- **Predictive Value:** Helps managers forecast employee responses to change.
- **Explanatory Value:** Provides reasons *why* employees behave in certain ways.
- **Prescriptive Value:** Offers actionable solutions to improve performance.
- **Self-Insight:** Helps employees understand their own behaviour better.
- **Organizational Effectiveness:** Direct link to productivity, profitability, and sustainability.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The MARS Model of Individual Behaviour (Core Theory)

The **MARS Model** is the most critical framework for understanding behaviour at work at the individual level. It is a KTU **favourite** for 7-mark and 14-mark questions.

$$B = f(M \cdot A \cdot R \cdot S)$$

Where:
- **B** = Behaviour
- **M** = Motivation (internal drives)
- **A** = Ability (capability to perform)
- **R** = Role perceptions (understanding job duties)
- **S** = Situational factors (environment, resources)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplicative Logic:** All four variables are **multiplied**, not added. This means if **any one factor is zero**, the entire behaviour becomes **zero**. A highly motivated employee with no ability produces no useful behaviour. This is a **highly tested KTU concept**.

### 2.2 Determinants of Individual Behaviour (Detailed Breakdown)

#### A. Biographical Characteristics
These are **objective, easily measurable** personal characteristics:
- **Age** (older workers tend to be more reliable, less absenteeism, but less physically agile)
- **Gender** (no significant difference in job performance per modern research)
- **Race & Religion** (diversity and inclusion considerations)
- **Tenure** (length of service correlates with experience)
- **Marital Status** (married employees often show lower turnover)

#### B. Ability — The Capacity to Perform
$$A = A_{Intellectual} + A_{Physical}$$

**Intellectual Abilities** (Mental Capacity):
- Number aptitude, verbal comprehension, perceptual speed
- Inductive reasoning, deductive reasoning, spatial visualization
- Memory (per KTU 2024, 7 dimensions are studied)

**Physical Abilities** (Body Capacity):
- Strength, stamina, flexibility, coordination

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Intellectual abilities = "**MIND**" tasks; Physical abilities = "**BODY**" tasks. Job-fit theory states that performance improves when **abilities match job requirements**.

#### C. Personality — The Unique Organization
Personality is the **sum total of ways** in which an individual reacts to and interacts with others. Determined by:

$$P = f(H, E, S)$$

Where **H** = Heredity, **E** = Environment, **S** = Situation.

**The Big Five Personality Traits (OCEAN Model) — KTU High-Yield:**

| Trait | Description | Workplace Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **O**penness | Curious, imaginative, creative | Innovative, welcomes change |
| **C**onscientiousness | Responsible, organized, reliable | High job performance across occupations |
| **E**xtraversion | Sociable, talkative, assertive | Strong sales performance |
| **A**greeableness | Trustworthy, cooperative, good-natured | Team player, low conflict |
| **N**euroticism | Anxious, insecure, emotional | Low job satisfaction, high turnover |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** "Openness" in personality theory means openness to **experience**, not political openness. Do not confuse.

#### D. Learning Theories (How Behaviour is Acquired)

| Theory | Mechanism | Key Figure | Workplace Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Classical Conditioning** | Stimulus-Response | Ivan Pavlov | Brand loyalty, advertising |
| **Operant Conditioning** | Reinforcement/Punishment | B.F. Skinner | Performance-based rewards |
| **Cognitive Learning** | Mental structuring | Edward Tolman | Problem-solving training |
| **Social Learning** | Observation & imitation | Albert Bandura | Mentoring, role modeling |

**Operant Conditioning Equation (for KTU):**

$$R_{future} = f(R_{type}, R_{schedule}, R_{magnitude})$$

Where **Reinforcement Types** are: Positive Reinforcement, Negative Reinforcement, Punishment, Extinction.

#### E. Attitudes and Job Satisfaction

An attitude has three components (KTU **must-know**):

$$A = (C_{cog}, C_{aff}, C_{beh})$$

- **Cognitive Component:** Beliefs and knowledge ("My pay is low")
- **Affective Component:** Feelings and emotions ("I feel angry about this")
- **Behavioural Component:** Intent to act ("I will look for another job")

> [!NOTE]
> **Cognitive Dissonance Theory (Leon Festinger):** When attitudes and behaviour are inconsistent, individuals experience psychological discomfort and try to resolve it. *Example:* An employee who values quality but produces shoddy work.

#### F. Perception Process

$$\text{Perception} = \text{Stimulus} \rightarrow \text{Attention} \rightarrow \text{Organization} \rightarrow \text{Interpretation} \rightarrow \text{Response}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attribution Theory (Fritz Heider):** We attribute behaviour to either **Internal Disposition** (personality) or **External Situation** (environment). KTU commonly asks: *"Explain attribution theory and its implications for OB."*

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Comprehensive)

| Concept | Formula/Framework | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Individual Behaviour | $B = f(M \cdot A \cdot R \cdot S)$ | Foundation model |
| Personality Determinants | $P = f(H, E, S)$ | Why people are different |
| MARS Behaviour Equation | Multiplicative M-A-R-S | All factors needed |
| Reinforcement Effect | $R_{future} = f(Behavior, Consequence)$ | Learning & training |
| Ability Composition | $A = A_{Intellectual} + A_{Physical}$ | Job-fit analysis |
| Attitude Tri-component | $A = (C_{cog}, C_{aff}, C_{beh})$ | Job satisfaction studies |
| Perception Stages | 5-stage pipeline | Manager decision-making |
| Big Five Traits | OCEAN acronym | Personality assessment |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Management

- **HR Analytics:** Using biographical data to predict turnover.
- **Performance Management:** Aligning ability with job design.
- **Learning & Development:** Designing operant conditioning-based training.
- **Conflict Resolution:** Understanding perception and attribution biases.
- **Team Building:** Matching personality types (e.g., MBTI, Big Five) to team roles.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Detailed Frameworks

### 3.1 The Complete MARS Model — Step-by-Step Expansion

**Step 1: Identify Motivation (M)**
Motivation is the *internal drive* to achieve a goal. It answers: **"Does the employee *want* to do it?"**

$$\text{Motivation} = \sum (\text{Intrinsic Rewards} + \text{Extrinsic Rewards})$$

- *Intrinsic:* Achievement, recognition, growth.
- *Extrinsic:* Pay, benefits, work conditions.

**Step 2: Assess Ability (A)**
Ability is the *capacity* to perform. It answers: **"Can the employee *do* it?"**

$$A = \int_{t=0}^{n} (I_{t} + P_{t}) \, dt$$

Where $I_t$ = intellectual ability at time $t$, $P_t$ = physical ability at time $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> The integration is conceptual — it shows that ability is a *continuous variable* developed over time, not a fixed state.

**Step 3: Clarify Role Perceptions (R)**
The employee's *understanding* of expected behaviour. It answers: **"Does the employee *know what* to do?"**

$$R = \frac{\text{Actual Understanding}}{\text{Expected Understanding}} \times 100\%$$

If R < 100%, **role ambiguity** exists, leading to poor performance.

**Step 4: Evaluate Situational Factors (S)**
External constraints like *resources, time, coworkers, and physical environment*. It answers: **"Are the conditions *conducive* for the behaviour?"**

$$S = f(R_{res}, T_{avail}, E_{culture}, C_{coworker})$$

**Step 5: Combine all factors (Multiplicative Synthesis)**

$$B_{effective} = M \times A \times R \times S$$

**Worked Numerical Example (for KTU Math-Enabled Questions):**

Suppose:
- $M = 0.8$ (high motivation)
- $A = 0.9$ (high ability)
- $R = 0.7$ (some role confusion)
- $S = 0.6$ (poor resources)

$$B = 0.8 \times 0.9 \times 0.7 \times 0.6 = 0.302$$

The effective behaviour is only **30.2%** of potential, even though motivation and ability are high. **Conclusion:** Improving $R$ and $S$ will have a *disproportionately large* impact.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Theories

| Theory | Originator | Core Premise | KTU-Standard Explanation | Criticism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hierarchy of Needs | Abraham Maslow | 5-tier pyramid of needs | Physiological → Safety → Social → Esteem → Self-Actualization | Needs are not strictly hierarchical |
| Two-Factor Theory | Frederick Herzberg | Hygiene + Motivators | Hygiene factors prevent dissatisfaction; motivators drive satisfaction | Limited sample (engineers) |
| Theory X & Y | Douglas McGregor | Managerial mindset | Theory X = pessimistic (people dislike work); Theory Y = optimistic (people seek responsibility) | Oversimplified |
| Expectancy Theory | Victor Vroom | Effort → Performance → Reward | $M = E \times I \times V$ (Motivation = Expectancy × Instrumentality × Valence) | Complex to apply |
| Equity Theory | J. Stacy Adams | Fairness comparison | $Output_A \div Input_A = Output_B \div Input_B$ | Individual perceptions vary |
| Goal-Setting Theory | Edwin Locke | Specific difficult goals | $P = f(G_{specific}, G_{commitment}, G_{feedback})$ | Doesn't account for ability |

### 3.3 The Vroom Expectancy Model — Full Derivation

**Step 1:** Motivation ($M$) depends on three beliefs.

**Step 2:** Expectancy ($E$) — belief that effort will lead to performance.

$$E = P(\text{Performance} \vert \text{Effort}), \quad 0 \le E \le 1$$

**Step 3:** Instrumentality ($I$) — belief that performance will lead to reward.

$$I = P(\text{Reward} \vert \text{Performance}), \quad 0 \le I \le 1$$

**Step 4:** Valence ($V$) — value placed on the reward.

$$V = f(\text{Personal Preference}), \quad -1 \le V \le 1$$

**Step 5:** Combine multiplicatively.

$$M = E \times I \times V$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** If *any* component is zero, motivation is zero. This is a **tested KTU 14-mark question pattern** — *explain expectancy theory with managerial implications*.

### 3.4 Adams' Equity Theory — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Individual compares their input-output ratio with a referent.

$$\text{Equity} = \frac{O_{self} \div I_{self}}{O_{referent} \div I_{referent}}$$

**Step 2:** Six possible scenarios:

| Scenario | Ratio Comparison | Employee Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| Equity | $O_{self}/I_{self} = O_{ref}/I_{ref}$ | Satisfied, no tension |
| Underpayment | $O_{self}/I_{self} < O_{ref}/I_{ref}$ | Anger, reduce effort, quit |
| Overpayment | $O_{self}/I_{self} > O_{ref}/I_{ref}$ | Guilt, may increase effort temporarily |

**Step 3:** Resolution Mechanisms (KTU Expected):
1. Change inputs (reduce effort)
2. Change outcomes (seek raise)
3. Change referent (compare with lower-paid peer)
4. Leave the field (quit)
5. Act on the referent (change their inputs)
6. Distort perceptions (rationalize)

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Behaviour at Work. Briefly explain the three levels of analysis in Organizational Behaviour.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Behaviour at Work refers to the systematic study of human behaviour in organizational settings, focusing on the actions and conduct of individuals, groups, and the organization as a whole **(1 mark)**. The three levels of analysis are:
1. **Individual Level** — Examines personal attributes like personality, perception, motivation, and attitudes **(1 mark)**.
2. **Group Level** — Studies group dynamics, norms, roles, and team behaviour **(0.5 marks)**.
3. **Organizational Level** — Analyzes organizational structure, culture, leadership, and overall design **(0.5 marks)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the MARS model of individual behaviour in organizations.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The MARS model states that individual behaviour is a function of four key factors: **M**otivation, **A**bility, **R**ole perceptions, and **S**ituational factors **(1 mark)**.
- **Motivation:** The internal drive to perform a task **(0.5 marks)**.
- **Ability:** The mental and physical capacity to perform **(0.5 marks)**.
- **Role Perceptions:** Understanding of expected job duties **(0.5 marks)**.
- **Situational Factors:** External conditions like resources and environment **(0.5 marks)**.

All four factors are **multiplicative**, meaning if any one is zero, behaviour is zero **(0.5 marks)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the Big Five personality traits (OCEAN model) and explain how each trait influences workplace behaviour. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Elaborate on Adams' Equity Theory of motivation with suitable workplace examples. Discuss the methods employees use to restore equity when they perceive inequity. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) The Big Five Personality Traits (OCEAN) — 7 Marks**

The Big Five is the most widely accepted framework for personality, comprising five core dimensions that capture most of the variation in human personality **(0.5 marks)**.

**1. Openness to Experience (1 Mark)**
- *Traits:* Imaginative, curious, creative, original.
- *Workplace Behaviour:* Open employees embrace change, generate innovative ideas, and adapt well to novel situations. They are valuable in R&D and creative roles.
- *Example:* An engineer at Google who proposes a new algorithm.

**2. Conscientiousness (1 Mark)**
- *Traits:* Responsible, organized, dependable, persistent.
- *Workplace Behaviour:* Conscientious employees show **strong job performance across nearly all occupations**. They are reliable, meet deadlines, and exhibit high citizenship behaviour.
- *Example:* An accountant who submits error-free reports on time.

**3. Extraversion (1 Mark)**
- *Traits:* Sociable, talkative, assertive, energetic.
- *Workplace Behaviour:* Extroverts perform strongly in **sales, leadership, and team-based roles**. Introverts excel in tasks requiring deep concentration.
- *Example:* A sales manager who energizes the team.

**4. Agreeableness (1 Mark)**
- *Traits:* Trustworthy, straightforward, good-natured, cooperative.
- *Workplace Behaviour:* Agreeable individuals are excellent team players, low in conflict, and helpful. They are valuable in customer service and HR.
- *Example:* A customer service executive who resolves complaints amicably.

**5. Neuroticism (1 Mark)**
- *Traits:* Anxious, insecure, emotional, depressed.
- *Workplace Behaviour:* High neuroticism correlates with **lower job satisfaction, higher turnover, and burnout**. It affects emotional stability under stress.
- *Example:* An employee unable to handle tight project deadlines.

**Personality and Job Fit (1.5 Marks)**
The **Personality-Job Fit Theory** by Holland states that an individual's personality should match the job's demands. For instance, **high conscientiousness** is desirable for accountants, while **high openness** is essential for designers. Mismatches lead to stress, poor performance, and turnover.

---

**(b) Adams' Equity Theory — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (1 Mark)**
J. Stacy Adams' **Equity Theory** (1963) posits that employees compare their input-output ratio with that of referent others. **Perceived inequity** leads to **tension**, which the employee is motivated to reduce.

**Core Proposition (1 Mark)**
The theory states:

$$\frac{O_{self}}{I_{self}} = \frac{O_{referent}}{I_{referent}} \quad \text{(Equity)}$$

Where $O$ = Outcomes (pay, recognition, benefits) and $I$ = Inputs (effort, skills, time).

**Workplace Example (1.5 Marks)**
*Situation:* Two software engineers, Riya and Sita, work in the same team. Riya has 5 years of experience (Sita has 3) and works 50 hours per week (Sita works 40). Both receive the same salary.
- Riya's ratio: $O/I = 100 / 50 = 2$
- Sita's ratio: $O/I = 100 / 40 = 2.5$
- **Inequity:** Riya perceives underpayment inequity.

**Methods to Restore Equity (3.5 Marks — the heart of the KTU answer):**
1. **Change Inputs (Own)** — Riya may reduce her working hours to 40.
2. **Change Outcomes (Own)** — Riya may request a salary raise or promotion.
3. **Change Referent** — Riya may compare herself to a less-experienced junior.
4. **Change Inputs (Referent)** — Riya may try to make Sita work harder.
5. **Change Outcomes (Referent)** — Riya may attempt to get Sita's salary reduced.
6. **Distort Perceptions** — Riya may rationalize that the job is more enjoyable.
7. **Leave the Field** — Riya may quit and join another company.
8. **Act on the Situation** — Riya may seek a transfer to another team.

**Managerial Implications (0.5 marks)**
Managers should ensure **fair, transparent reward systems**, conduct regular pay equity audits, and communicate openly about compensation decisions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the MARS model of individual behaviour with a real-world example. Why is this model called "multiplicative" and what is its significance? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss Vroom's Expectancy Theory of motivation. Derive the formula and explain its managerial implications with examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) MARS Model — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 Mark)**
The MARS model, proposed in contemporary OB literature, explains that individual behaviour in organizations is a function of **four critical factors**: Motivation, Ability, Role perceptions, and Situational factors. Mathematically:

$$B = M \times A \times R \times S$$

**Detailed Components (4 Marks)**
- **Motivation (M) (1 Mark):** The internal force that energizes behaviour. High motivation is necessary for the employee to *want* to perform. Example: An employee motivated by promotion works harder.
- **Ability (A) (1 Mark):** The mental and physical capability to perform. Includes both intellectual and physical abilities. Example: A programmer with strong coding skills.
- **Role Perceptions (R) (1 Mark):** The employee's understanding of *what* they are supposed to do. Role ambiguity here reduces behaviour. Example: A new joiner unsure of KPIs.
- **Situational Factors (S) (1 Mark):** External conditions, including resources, time, and environment. Example: Slow internet reduces developer productivity.

**Real-World Example (1 Mark)**
A software developer is highly motivated to deliver a project (M=0.9), has excellent coding skills (A=0.9), clearly understands the requirements (R=0.8), but the company provides outdated hardware (S=0.5). The behaviour is:

$$B = 0.9 \times 0.9 \times 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.324$$

**Multiplicative Significance and Conclusion (1 Mark)**
All four factors are multiplied. This means a single weak factor can drastically reduce behaviour. Managers must address **all four** dimensions rather than just one (e.g., hiring skilled employees but ignoring motivation yields poor results).

---

**(b) Vroom's Expectancy Theory — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (1 Mark)**
Victor Vroom's **Expectancy Theory** (1964) focuses on the **cognitive process** underlying motivation. It states that motivation is a function of three beliefs: Expectancy, Instrumentality, and Valence.

**Derivation of the Formula (2.5 Marks)**
Step 1: Let $E$ = Expectancy, the belief that effort leads to performance.

$$E = P(\text{Performance} \vert \text{Effort}), \quad 0 \le E \le 1$$

Step 2: Let $I$ = Instrumentality, the belief that performance leads to a reward.

$$I = P(\text{Reward} \vert \text{Performance}), \quad 0 \le I \le 1$$

Step 3: Let $V$ = Valence, the value the individual places on the reward.

$$V = f(\text{Personal Preference}), \quad -1 \le V \le +1$$

Step 4: Combine multiplicatively.

$$M = E \times I \times V$$

**Worked Example (1.5 Marks)**
Consider a sales executive:
- Expectancy ($E$): Believes working hard leads to meeting targets, $E = 0.8$.
- Instrumentality ($I$): Believes meeting targets will earn a bonus, $I = 0.7$.
- Valence ($V$): Values the bonus highly, $V = 0.9$.

$$M = 0.8 \times 0.7 \times 0.9 = 0.504$$

The motivation score is **0.504**, indicating moderate motivation.

**Managerial Implications (2 Marks)**
1. **Build Expectancy:** Provide training, resources, and clear job design so that employees believe effort yields performance.
2. **Build Instrumentality:** Establish clear performance-reward linkages, transparent performance appraisals, and reliable reward systems.
3. **Increase Valence:** Offer rewards that are individually meaningful — not all employees value money equally; some prefer flexible hours, recognition, or growth.
4. **Personalize Rewards:** Use surveys and 1-on-1 discussions to understand individual valences.
5. **Trust and Fairness:** If employees distrust the system, $E$, $I$, and $V$ all collapse, and so does motivation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "Openness"** in the Big Five with political openness. Always clarify "Openness to Experience."
> 2. **MARS is multiplicative, not additive.** Writing $B = M + A + R + S$ is a **direct 1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Equity Theory is about *perception*, not reality.** Always emphasize "perceived" inequity in your answer.
> 4. **Vroom's formula requires valence to range from -1 to +1**, not 0 to 1, unlike expectancy and instrumentality. Many students miss this.
> 5. **Always link theory to workplace examples.** KTU examiners award bonus marks (sometimes 1-2 marks) for relevant, real-world illustrations.
> 6. **For Personality, mention Heredity, Environment, and Situation as the three determinants** — this is a common 2-mark sub-question.
> 7. **For 14-mark questions, structure your answer with diagrams/headings** — students who present a wall of text lose marks on presentation.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Behaviour at Work**

- **Core Definition:** Behaviour at Work = Study of human behaviour in organizational settings (Individual + Group + Organization).
- **Three Levels:** Individual → Group → Organizational (I-G-O).
- **MARS Model:** $B = M \times A \times R \times S$ (multiplicative; remember all four are necessary).
- **Personality Determinants:** Heredity, Environment, Situation.
- **Big Five (OCEAN):** Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism. **Conscientiousness** is the strongest predictor of job performance.
- **Ability:** Intellectual (MIND) + Physical (BODY).
- **Attitude Tri-component:** Cognitive + Affective + Behavioural.
- **Cognitive Dissonance:** Mismatch between attitude and behaviour causes discomfort.
- **Attribution Theory:** Internal (disposition) vs External (situation) attribution.
- **Classical Conditioning:** Pavlov (stimulus-response).
- **Operant Conditioning:** Skinner (4 types: positive/negative reinforcement, punishment, extinction).
- **Social Learning:** Bandura (observation and imitation, self-efficacy).
- **Equity Theory (Adams):** $O_{self}/I_{self} = O_{ref}/I_{ref}$; inequity causes tension; 6+ ways to restore equity.
- **Expectancy Theory (Vroom):** $M = E \times I \times V$; effort → performance → reward.
- **Goal-Setting Theory (Locke):** Specific, difficult goals + commitment + feedback = high performance.
- **Maslow's Hierarchy:** 5 levels (physiological, safety, social, esteem, self-actualization).
- **Herzberg's Two-Factor:** Hygiene factors + Motivators.
- **Theory X vs Y:** McGregor's managerial assumptions.
- **Perception Process:** 5 stages — attention, organization, interpretation, etc.
- **Key Mnemonic for Three Levels:** **I-G-O** = Individual, Group, Organization.
- **KTU 2024 Favourite Topics:** MARS Model, Big Five, Equity Theory, Expectancy Theory.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Organization Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT418 - ORGANISATIONAL BEHAVIOUR
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Organization Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Organisational Behaviour (OB)** is the systematic study of the structure, functioning, and performance of human behaviour within organisational settings, along with the application of this knowledge to improve an organisation's effectiveness. It is an interdisciplinary field that draws from psychology, sociology, anthropology, political science, and economics to understand, predict, and influence individual, group, and organisational-level outcomes.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, OB is positioned as a behavioural science that examines three interdependent levels of analysis:

- **Micro Level** (Individual behaviour)
- **Meso Level** (Group and team behaviour)
- **Macro Level** (Organisational system behaviour)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** OB is NOT just "common sense about people." It is a *scientific discipline* that uses empirical research methods, validated theories, and measurable outcomes to explain workplace behaviour. Examiners frequently award or deduct marks based on this distinction.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy: The Symphony Orchestra**

Think of an organisation as a **symphony orchestra**:

| Orchestra Element | Organisation Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Individual musicians | Employees with skills, attitudes, personalities |
| Section leaders (violins, brass) | Team leaders and supervisors |
| Conductor | Top management / leadership |
| Sheet music | Rules, policies, organisational culture |
| Audience response | Organisational performance and stakeholder satisfaction |

A great orchestra is not produced by hiring only virtuoso soloists. It requires **coordination, shared norms, role clarity, leadership, and a unifying culture**. Similarly, a great organisation is not just a collection of talented individuals—it is a *coordinated social system* where behaviour, structure, and process interact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** OB studies the *interaction* between people and the organisation, not just people in isolation. Behaviour is shaped by individual traits **AND** situational forces.

---

## 1.3 Why Study Organisational Behaviour?

Three concrete reasons that KTU examiners expect you to articulate:

1. **To explain behaviour** – Understand *why* employees behave the way they do (motivation, perception, personality).
2. **To predict behaviour** – Forecast how people will react to change, conflict, or leadership styles.
3. **To influence behaviour** – Shape attitudes, motivation, and performance towards organisational goals.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorisation Shortcut:** The **E-P-I Triad** — **Explain → Predict → Influence** — is a high-yield answer for any 3-mark "Why study OB?" type question.

---

## 1.4 Nature and Characteristics of OB

- **Behavioural science orientation** – Focuses on human behaviour, not technical systems.
- **Interdisciplinary** – Integrates multiple social sciences.
- **Applied field** – Theory is directly tied to managerial practice.
- **Humanistic orientation** – Treats people as assets, not costs.
- **Normative science** – Prescribes what *ought* to be done, not just what is.
- **Goal-oriented** – Ultimately aims at improving organisational effectiveness and individual well-being.

---

## 1.5 Scope of Organisational Behaviour

OB encompasses behaviour at **three analytical levels**:

$$
\text{Scope of OB} = \underbrace{\text{Individual Level}}_{\text{Personality, Perception, Motivation, Attitudes}} \cup \underbrace{\text{Group Level}}_{\text{Team dynamics, Communication, Leadership, Power, Conflict}} \cup \underbrace{\text{Organisational Level}}_{\text{Structure, Culture, Change, Design, Environment}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently frames questions around the **three levels of analysis**. Always structure your answer using this tri-level framework to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Contributing Disciplines to OB

OB is a *convergent* discipline. Each contributing field provides a unique lens:

| Contributing Discipline | Contribution to OB | Unit of Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| Psychology | Personality, perception, learning, motivation, attitudes | Individual |
| Sociology | Group dynamics, organisational structure, bureaucracy, culture | Group / Organisation |
| Anthropology | Cross-cultural behaviour, values, organisational rituals | Organisation / Society |
| Political Science | Power, conflict, negotiation, organisational politics | Group / Organisation |
| Economics | Job design, reward systems, performance-economic trade-offs | Individual / Organisation |
| Social Psychology | Interpersonal behaviour, group decision-making | Individual in groups |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** **"P-S-A-P-E"** → **P**sychology, **S**ociology, **A**nthropology, **P**olitical Science, **E**conomics.

---

## 2.2 The Three-Level OB Framework (Detailed Breakdown)

### Level 1 — Individual Behaviour
Variables studied:
- **Personality** – The unique psychological makeup that influences response patterns.
- **Perception** – The process of interpreting environmental stimuli.
- **Motivation** – Internal drives that activate and direct behaviour.
- **Attitudes** – Evaluative statements (cognitive, affective, behavioural components).
- **Emotions and Moods** – Affective reactions to events.
- **Learning** – Relatively permanent change in behaviour due to experience.

### Level 2 — Group Behaviour
Variables studied:
- **Group formation and development** (Tuckman's forming-storming-norming-performing-adjourning).
- **Team effectiveness** and team design.
- **Communication patterns** (formal/informal, upward/downward/horizontal).
- **Leadership styles** (autocratic, democratic, laissez-faire, transformational).
- **Power and politics** in organisations.
- **Conflict and negotiation** processes.

### Level 3 — Organisational System Behaviour
Variables studied:
- **Organisational structure** (functional, divisional, matrix, network).
- **Organisational culture** (artefacts, values, assumptions).
- **Organisational change and development**.
- **Organisational design** and work specialisation.
- **Organisational environment** and its interface with the system.

---

## 2.3 Key OB Models and Frameworks

### 2.3.1 The S-O-B-C Model (Stimulus–Organism–Behaviour–Consequence)

This is the foundational behavioural model:

$$
S \rightarrow O \rightarrow B \rightarrow C
$$

Where:
- **S** = Stimulus (the environmental trigger or input)
- **O** = Organism (the individual's internal state — personality, perception, motivation)
- **B** = Behaviour (the observable action)
- **C** = Consequence (the outcome or feedback)

**Managerial Insight:** To change behaviour (B), a manager can alter the **S** (job design, rewards), the **O** (training, attitudes), or the **C** (feedback, reinforcement).

### 2.3.2 The MARS Model of Individual Behaviour

OB scholarship widely adopts the MARS model, where four factors jointly determine individual behaviour and results:

$$
\text{Results} = f(M, A, R, S)
$$

Where:
- **M** = Motivation
- **A** = Ability
- **R** = Role perceptions
- **S** = Situational factors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** If *any* one of the four MARS factors is missing or weak, performance will suffer — even if the other three are strong. This is a high-frequency 7-mark essay topic.

### 2.3.3 The Big Five Personality Framework (OCEAN)

For individual-level questions on personality:

| Trait | Description |
|---|---|
| **O**penness | Curiosity, creativity, preference for variety |
| **C**onscientiousness | Self-discipline, organisation, goal-directed behaviour |
| **E**xtraversion | Sociability, assertiveness, positive emotions |
| **A**greeableness | Cooperative, trusting, considerate |
| **N**euroticism | Emotional instability, anxiety, moodiness |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table

> All key concepts consolidated for last-minute revision:

| Concept | Definition | Key Use in OB |
|---|---|---|
| Organisational Behaviour | Systematic study of human behaviour in orgs | Foundation of the subject |
| Individual Level | Behaviour of a single person | Personality, motivation, perception |
| Group Level | Behaviour in teams/groups | Leadership, communication, power |
| Organisational Level | Behaviour of the whole system | Structure, culture, change |
| S-O-B-C Model | Stimulus-Organism-Behaviour-Consequence | Predicting and managing behaviour |
| MARS Model | Motivation-Ability-Role-Situation | Explaining individual performance |
| Big Five (OCEAN) | Five-trait personality model | Personality and work outcomes |
| Hawthorne Effect | Behaviour changes when observed | Research methodology in OB |
| Contingency Approach | There is no single best way to manage | Core OB philosophy |
| E-P-I Triad | Explain-Predict-Influence | Justification for studying OB |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse:** OB is *descriptive* (what people do) AND *prescriptive* (what managers should do). Most undergraduates miss the prescriptive component in Part A answers.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of OB

- **Recruitment and Selection** – Using personality assessments to improve person-job fit.
- **Performance Management** – Aligning motivation theory with reward systems.
- **Leadership Development** – Applying leadership theories to train managers.
- **Organisational Change** – Managing resistance using OB insights.
- **Conflict Resolution** – Applying negotiation and power frameworks.
- **Diversity Management** – Building inclusive workplaces using cultural anthropology.

---

## 2.6 Challenges and Opportunities for OB

| Challenges | Opportunities |
|---|---|
| Workforce diversity | Innovation through varied perspectives |
| Globalisation | Cross-cultural management research |
| Rapid technological change | Remote work, virtual teams research |
| Employee disengagement | Positive organisational scholarship |
| Ethical lapses in business | Stronger ethical climate frameworks |
| Generational differences | Customised motivation strategies |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Concept Expansion

> Since this is a conceptual, non-quantitative topic, the "derivation" here is replaced by a **structured analytical walkthrough** of the OB framework — the way a topper would articulate it in a 14-mark KTU answer.

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: From Concept to Application

### Step 1 — Identify the Focal Unit of Analysis

The first analytical step is to ask: **At which level is the behaviour occurring?**

$$
\text{Behaviour} = f(\text{Individual}, \text{Group}, \text{Organisation})
$$

- If a single employee is dissatisfied with pay → **Individual level** (use motivation theory).
- If a project team cannot meet deadlines → **Group level** (use team dynamics).
- If two departments constantly clash → **Organisational level** (use structure and culture).

### Step 2 — Diagnose Using a Behavioural Model

Apply the **MARS model** to diagnose the cause of poor performance:

$$
\text{Poor Performance} \Leftarrow \text{low } M, \text{ low } A, \text{ low } R, \text{ low } S
$$

**Diagnostic Logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2a:} \quad &\text{Check } M \text{ (Motivation)}: \text{Is the employee willing to work?} \\
\text{Step 2b:} \quad &\text{Check } A \text{ (Ability)}: \text{Does the employee have the skills?} \\
\text{Step 2c:} \quad &\text{Check } R \text{ (Role Perceptions)}: \text{Does the employee know what to do?} \\
\text{Step 2d:} \quad &\text{Check } S \text{ (Situational Factors)}: \text{Are tools, time, and conditions adequate?}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Each step leads to a distinct managerial intervention:
> - Low M → redesign rewards, clarify goals.
> - Low A → training, recruitment review.
> - Low R → clearer job descriptions, performance coaching.
> - Low S → remove obstacles, supply resources.

### Step 3 — Apply the S-O-B-C Framework for Intervention

For each identified gap, design an intervention along the S-O-B-C chain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow \text{Change the stimulus (job design, work environment, reward)} \\
O &\rightarrow \text{Change the organism (training, attitude change)} \\
B &\rightarrow \text{Shape the behaviour (reinforcement, modelling)} \\
C &\rightarrow \text{Adjust consequences (feedback, discipline)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate Using the Contingency Approach

OB does not endorse universal solutions. The contingency approach states:

$$
\text{Best Management Practice} = f(\text{Situation})
$$

There is **no single best way** to lead, structure, or motivate. The right answer depends on the employee's maturity, the team's stage, the organisation's environment, and the technology used.

### Step 5 — Measure Outcomes

The final step is outcome evaluation across **four core outcomes of OB**:

$$
\text{OB Effectiveness} = f(P, C, S, E)
$$

Where:
- **P** = Productivity (output per unit input)
- **C** = Customer satisfaction (quality from the external stakeholder's view)
- **S** = Survival (long-term organisational viability)
- **E** = Employee well-being (job satisfaction, engagement, work-life balance)

> [!TIP]
> A top-scoring KTU answer ends with a clear mention of the **quadruple bottom line** — productivity, customer satisfaction, survival, and employee well-being.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Discipline-wise Contribution

| OB Variable | Psychological Lens | Sociological Lens | Anthropological Lens | Political Lens |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Motivation | Need theories (Maslow, Herzberg) | Cultural expectations of work | Cross-cultural reward preferences | Power and reward allocation |
| Leadership | Trait and behavioural theories | Role expectations in groups | Cultural leadership prototypes | Influence and authority bases |
| Communication | Cognitive processing | Network structures | Cultural communication styles | Negotiation and bargaining |
| Conflict | Emotional triggers | Inter-group dynamics | Cultural conflict norms | Power contests, political behaviour |

> This matrix demonstrates to the examiner that you understand OB as a *convergent* field, not a single-discipline view.

---

## 3.3 Historical Evolution (Analytical Timeline)

| Era | Key Movement | Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| Late 1800s | **Scientific Management** (Taylor) | Task specialisation, efficiency |
| 1920s–1930s | **Hawthorne Studies** | Social factors matter; human relations |
| 1930s–1940s | **Human Relations Movement** | Emphasis on employee needs |
| 1950s–1960s | **Behavioural Science Approach** | Theory X/Y, motivation-hygiene theory |
| 1970s–1980s | **Contingency & Systems Approach** | No one best way |
| 1990s onwards | **OB in the Global Era** | Diversity, ethics, technology, change |
| 2010s onwards | **Positive OB, Agile, Remote Work** | Well-being, engagement, virtual teams |

> [!NOTE]
> The shift from **mechanistic** to **humanistic** to **contingent** to **positive** OB is a recurring theme in KTU Module 1 questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: Define Organisational Behaviour. State any two of its characteristics. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model pattern]`

**Model Answer:**

Organisational Behaviour is the systematic study of the structure, functioning, and performance of human behaviour in organisational settings. It examines how individuals, groups, and structures affect behaviour, and applies this knowledge to improve organisational effectiveness.

**Two Characteristics:**
1. **Interdisciplinary Nature** – OB draws from psychology, sociology, anthropology, political science, and economics.
2. **Humanistic Orientation** – OB treats employees as valuable human assets whose well-being is central to productivity.

---

### Question 2: List the three levels of analysis in OB and give one variable studied at each level. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model pattern]`

**Model Answer:**

The three levels of analysis in OB are:

1. **Individual Level** – Variables: *Personality* (e.g., Big Five traits), motivation, perception, attitudes.
2. **Group Level** – Variables: *Leadership styles* (e.g., autocratic, democratic), group dynamics, power.
3. **Organisational Level** – Variables: *Organisational culture* (artefacts, values, assumptions), structure, change.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** "Organisational Behaviour is the study of what people do, why they do it, and what impact it has on organisational performance." Critically analyse this statement. Discuss the three levels of OB and the contributing disciplines.

#### Part (a) — The Three Levels of OB [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

OB is structured around three interconnected levels:

**1. Individual Level (Micro)**
Behaviour of a single person in an organisation. Variables include personality, perception, motivation, attitudes, learning, and emotions. Example: An employee with high conscientiousness will display greater self-discipline and reliability.

**2. Group Level (Meso)**
Behaviour of two or more interacting people. Variables include group dynamics, leadership, communication, power, politics, and conflict. Example: A self-managed team in an IT firm demonstrates shared leadership and peer accountability.

**3. Organisational Level (Macro)**
Behaviour of the organisation as a whole. Variables include structure, design, culture, change, and environment. Example: Google's flat organisational structure encourages innovation and rapid decision-making.

$$
\text{Three Levels of OB} = \text{Individual} \cup \text{Group} \cup \text{Organisation}
$$

Each level affects and is affected by the others. An individual personality (Level 1) shapes team behaviour (Level 2), which in turn is shaped by organisational culture (Level 3).

#### Part (b) — Contributing Disciplines [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

OB is a convergent discipline. The major contributing fields are:

1. **Psychology** – Studies individual behaviour. Contributes theories of personality, motivation (Maslow, Herzberg), perception, and learning.

2. **Sociology** – Studies social systems. Contributes knowledge of group dynamics, organisational structure, bureaucracy, and culture.

3. **Anthropology** – Studies cultures. Contributes understanding of cross-cultural behaviour, rituals, values, and organisational symbolism.

4. **Political Science** – Studies power. Contributes theories of authority, influence, organisational politics, and negotiation.

5. **Economics** – Studies resource allocation. Contributes theories of job design, wage determination, and reward-performance trade-offs.

> [!TIP]
> **Conclude with:** OB synthesises these disciplines because no single field can fully explain workplace behaviour. The interdisciplinary integration is OB's *core strength*.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** Explain the MARS model and the S-O-B-C model of individual behaviour. How do they help managers diagnose and influence performance?

#### Part (a) — The MARS Model [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The **MARS Model** states that individual performance is the joint function of four factors:

$$
\text{Performance} = f(M, A, R, S)
$$

| Factor | Meaning | Managerial Action |
|---|---|---|
| **M**otivation | Internal forces that drive effort | Goal-setting, rewards, recognition |
| **A**bility | Capability and skill | Recruitment, training, mentoring |
| **R**ole Perceptions | Understanding of job duties | Clear job descriptions, feedback |
| **S**ituational Factors | External conditions, resources, tools | Equipment, support, time allocation |

**Diagnostic Logic:**
- A trained, motivated employee with unclear instructions will underperform (low R).
- A clear-job employee lacking skills will underperform (low A).
- Hence, all four factors must be aligned for sustained performance.

#### Part (b) — The S-O-B-C Model and Managerial Use [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The **S-O-B-C Model** is a behavioural chain:

$$
S \rightarrow O \rightarrow B \rightarrow C
$$

- **Stimulus** – The environmental trigger (e.g., a new work policy).
- **Organism** – The individual receiving the stimulus (their attitudes, personality, prior experience).
- **Behaviour** – The observable response (e.g., acceptance or resistance).
- **Consequence** – The outcome, which feeds back into the organism (e.g., reward, punishment, recognition).

**Managerial Use:**
1. To **predict** behaviour — by studying O, managers can anticipate responses to new S.
2. To **influence** behaviour — by altering C (consequences), managers reinforce desired B.
3. To **explain** behaviour — by tracing the chain, the root cause of resistance is identified.
4. To **change** behaviour — by modifying S (job redesign) or O (training), behaviour shifts over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MARS and S-O-B-C models are **complementary** — MARS explains the *causes* of performance, while S-O-B-C explains the *process* of behaviour. Together they form the diagnostic backbone of OB.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in OB Answers:**
>
> 1. **Vague definitions** — Writing "OB is about behaviour" without mentioning *systematic study* and *organisational effectiveness* will lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Missing the multi-level framework** — Discussing only individual or only organisational behaviour is incomplete. Always use the **3-level framework**.
> 3. **Ignoring the E-P-I triad** — Many students justify studying OB only as "useful" but forget to mention **Explain, Predict, Influence**.
> 4. **Confusing MARS with Big Five** — MARS is a *performance* model; Big Five is a *personality* model. Examiners catch this mismatch.
> 5. **Skipping contributing disciplines** — Naming "psychology" alone is insufficient. The expected answer is at least **P-S-A-P-E** (Psychology, Sociology, Anthropology, Political Science, Economics).
> 6. **No real-world example** — A purely theoretical answer scores lower. A single industry or company reference boosts the mark by 1–2.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **OB Definition** = Systematic study + human behaviour + organisational setting + improving effectiveness.
- **Three Levels** = Individual → Group → Organisation.
- **E-P-I Triad** = Explain, Predict, Influence (justification for studying OB).
- **P-S-A-P-E** = Psychology, Sociology, Anthropology, Political Science, Economics (contributing disciplines).
- **MARS Model** = Motivation + Ability + Role Perceptions + Situational factors = Performance.
- **S-O-B-C Model** = Stimulus → Organism → Behaviour → Consequence.
- **Big Five** = OCEAN (Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism).
- **Contingency Approach** = There is no single best way; it depends on the situation.
- **Four Outcomes of OB** = Productivity, Customer Satisfaction, Survival, Employee Well-being.
- **Nature of OB** = Behavioural, Interdisciplinary, Humanistic, Applied, Normative, Goal-oriented.
- **Hawthorne Effect** = Behaviour changes when people know they are being observed.
- **Always end answers** with practical managerial implications and a real-world example to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Major Behavioural Science Disciplines Contributing to OB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT418 - ORGANISATIONAL BEHAVIOUR
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Major Behavioural Science Disciplines Contributing to OB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Organisational Behaviour (OB)

**Organisational Behaviour (OB)** is the systematic study and application of knowledge about how individuals, groups, and structures affect and are affected by behaviour within organisations. It is an interdisciplinary field that draws its theoretical foundations from multiple behavioural science disciplines to predict, explain, and manage human behaviour in workplace settings.

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, OB is defined as:
> A field of study that investigates the impact that individuals, groups, and structure have on behaviour within organisations, for the purpose of applying such knowledge toward improving an organisation's effectiveness.

### 1.2 Behavioural Science Disciplines

The term **"Behavioural Sciences"** refers to those disciplines that deal with the systematic study of human and animal behaviour through controlled observation, experimentation, and analysis. In the context of OB, the major contributing disciplines are:

1. **Psychology**
2. **Sociology**
3. **Anthropology**
4. **Social Psychology**
5. **Political Science**
6. **Economics**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The unit-level focus is on understanding the **interdisciplinary nature of OB**. Students must be able to identify the contribution of **each** behavioural science discipline and link it to OB variables (Individual, Group, Organisation System).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Body Hospital**
> Imagine a hospital treating a complex human body. No single doctor (no single discipline) can cure the patient alone. The **Cardiologist** (Psychology) studies the heart of behaviour — individual mind, perception, motivation. The **Orthopaedic Surgeon** (Sociology) examines the skeleton — group structures, social systems. The **Dermatologist** (Anthropology) looks at the outer skin — culture, customs, traditions. The **Neurologist** (Social Psychology) studies signals between parts — communication, attitudes. The **Nephrologist** (Economics) balances the fluids of the body — resource allocation, decision-making. The **Cardiothoracic Surgeon** (Political Science) handles power and conflict dynamics. Together, they form a **multi-specialty hospital** — that is **Organisational Behaviour**.

### 1.4 Why OB is Interdisciplinary

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Behaviour is complex** | Human behaviour cannot be explained by a single discipline. |
| **Multi-level analysis** | OB studies individuals, groups, and organisations — different levels need different lenses. |
| **Holistic understanding** | Real workplace problems span psychological, social, cultural, political, and economic dimensions. |
| **Better managerial decisions** | Combining insights produces more accurate predictions and effective interventions. |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Term to Remember for KTU Exams:** *Behavioural science disciplines contributing to OB = the academic roots that OB borrows from to become a holistic, applied social science.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Contributing Discipline

#### 2.1.1 Psychology
- **Definition:** The science that studies **individual human behaviour and mental processes** to understand why people act, think, and feel the way they do.
- **Origin / Father:** Wilhelm Wundt (1879) — first formal psychology laboratory.
- **Key Contributors to OB knowledge:** learning, perception, personality, motivation, emotions, attitudes, training, leadership effectiveness, job satisfaction, employee selection, performance appraisal, stress management, organisational development.
- **Levels of Analysis:** Micro-level (individual).
- **Why Psychology matters to OB:** The individual is the smallest unit of analysis in OB. Psychology provides the deepest insight into individual differences, which directly influence workplace outcomes.
- **Major Sub-branches used in OB:**
   * Industrial/Organisational Psychology (I-O Psychology)
   * Cognitive Psychology
   * Clinical Psychology (for stress and emotional disorders at work)
   * Personality Psychology

#### 2.1.2 Sociology
- **Definition:** The science that studies **people, their relationships, and the collective behaviours of social systems, institutions, and societies**.
- **Key Contributors to OB knowledge:** group dynamics, organisational structure, organisational culture, formal/informal organisations, bureaucracy, communication networks, status, role theory.
- **Levels of Analysis:** Macro-level (organisation and society).
- **Why Sociology matters to OB:** People do not work in isolation. Their behaviour is shaped by group memberships, hierarchies, and social systems. Sociology helps managers understand collective behaviour, organisational design, and cross-cultural differences.
- **Major Sub-branches used in OB:**
   * Industrial Sociology
   * Organisational Sociology
   * Sociometry (study of social relationships)

#### 2.1.3 Social Psychology
- **Definition:** The branch of psychology that focuses on **the effect of other people (real, imagined, or implied) on an individual's behaviour, attitudes, and beliefs**.
- **Key Contributors to OB knowledge:** behavioural change, attitude change, communication, group processes, group decision-making, power, conflict, negotiation, leadership, persuasion.
- **Levels of Analysis:** Bridge between micro (individual) and meso (group) levels.
- **Why Social Psychology matters to OB:** Most workplace behaviour occurs in the presence of others. Social psychology blends individual psychology with group sociology — making it the perfect **bridge discipline** for OB.
- **Major Concepts:** conformity, obedience, social loafing, groupthink, social facilitation, persuasion (Yale approach), cognitive dissonance, attribution theory.

#### 2.1.4 Anthropology
- **Definition:** The study of **societies, especially their cultures, origins, evolution, and physical characteristics of human populations** — the most macro of the behavioural sciences.
- **Key Contributors to OB knowledge:** organisational culture, rituals, ceremonies, values, norms, cross-cultural comparisons, organisational symbols, subcultures, ethnography.
- **Levels of Analysis:** Macro-level (societies and cultures, both ancient and modern).
- **Why Anthropology matters to OB:** With globalisation, organisations have culturally diverse workforces. Understanding rituals, symbols, and unwritten cultural rules is essential. Anthropology brings the "deep-time" and "deep-culture" perspective.
- **Major Sub-branches used in OB:**
   * Cultural Anthropology
   * Ethno-methodology
   * Comparative Management (studying managerial practices across cultures)

#### 2.1.5 Political Science
- **Definition:** The study of **the distribution and transfer of power, governmental systems, and political behaviour** of individuals and groups.
- **Key Contributors to OB knowledge:** power dynamics, conflict, negotiation, politics, allocation of resources, influence tactics, lobbying, ethics, organisational governance.
- **Levels of Analysis:** Macro-level (organisation as a political system).
- **Why Political Science matters to OB:** Every organisation is a political arena where scarce resources are competed for. Understanding power, influence, and conflict helps managers navigate organisational realities.

#### 2.1.6 Economics
- **Definition:** The science that studies **how individuals, organisations, and societies allocate scarce resources among competing wants**.
- **Key Contributors to OB knowledge:** decision-making under scarcity, compensation, wage theory, reward systems, cost-benefit analysis, human capital theory, behavioural economics, labour-market dynamics.
- **Levels of Analysis:** Both micro (individual) and macro (organisational).
- **Why Economics matters to OB:** All managerial decisions involve trade-offs. Economic theories of motivation (e.g., agency theory, expectancy theory — partly economic), pay structures, and rational choice apply directly to OB.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Discipline-to-OB Variable Mapping

| Discipline | Level of Analysis | Major OB Contribution | Key OB Variable Studied |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Psychology** | Individual | Learning, perception, motivation, personality | Individual behaviour |
| **Sociology** | Group / Organisation | Group dynamics, structure, bureaucracy | Group behaviour |
| **Social Psychology** | Individual ↔ Group | Communication, attitudes, conflict | Interpersonal behaviour |
| **Anthropology** | Organisation / Society | Culture, rituals, values | Organisational culture |
| **Political Science** | Organisation | Power, conflict, politics | Organisational politics |
| **Economics** | Individual / Organisation | Decision-making, rewards, scarcity | Organisational system |

### 2.3 Common Threads Across Disciplines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Thread #1 — Focus on Human Behaviour:** All six disciplines ultimately aim to describe, predict, and explain human behaviour.
>
> **Common Thread #2 — Empirical/Research-Based:** All rely on scientific methods — observation, experimentation, data analysis.
>
> **Common Thread #3 — Systematically Building Knowledge:** Each builds verifiable, testable, and cumulative bodies of knowledge.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Management

| OB Managerial Task | Discipline(s) Drawn From |
|---|---|
| Designing a fair **compensation system** | Economics + Psychology |
| Resolving **inter-group conflict** | Sociology + Social Psychology + Political Science |
| Building an inclusive **organisational culture** | Anthropology + Sociology |
| Conducting a **job interview / selection** | Psychology |
| Negotiating a **union contract** | Political Science + Economics |
| Conducting **cross-cultural training** for global managers | Anthropology + Social Psychology |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Contribution Matrix

### 3.1 Logical Derivation — Why OB is a Synthesised Discipline

Let us now derive, step by step, **how OB emerges as the integrated output** of these disciplines.

**Step 1 — Recognise the Core Problem.**
The behaviour of employees in an organisation is influenced by:
- Their **mind** (thoughts, feelings, motivations).
- Their **group memberships** (teams, departments, unions).
- Their **organisational context** (structure, culture, politics).
- Their **external environment** (society, economy, culture, government).

**Step 2 — Realise the Limits of a Single Discipline.**
No single behavioural science can fully explain all four influences. Example: Psychology alone cannot explain group behaviour; Sociology alone cannot explain individual motivation.

**Step 3 — Borrow Conceptually from Each Discipline.**
Each contributing discipline offers a unique lens:

$$
\text{Individual Insight} = f(\text{Psychology}, \text{Economics})
$$

$$
\text{Group Insight} = f(\text{Sociology}, \text{Social Psychology})
$$

$$
\text{Organisational Insight} = f(\text{Anthropology}, \text{Political Science}, \text{Sociology})
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate the Insights at Three OB Levels.**

$$
\text{OB Framework} = f(\text{Individual Level}, \text{Group Level}, \text{Organisation-System Level})
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Scientific Method to Test the Integrated Insights.**
OB borrows the research methods (experiments, surveys, case studies) from these disciplines to validate its theories.

**Step 6 — Produce Actionable Managerial Knowledge.**
The end result is a body of **applied** knowledge — guidelines for managers on leadership, motivation, communication, structure, and culture.

### 3.2 The Contribution Matrix — Discipline × OB Level

The following matrix maps each contributing discipline to the three OB levels of analysis. A tick (✓) indicates a primary contribution; a dot (●) indicates a secondary contribution.

| Discipline | Individual Level | Group Level | Organisation-System Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Psychology** | ✓ (Primary) | ● | ● |
| **Sociology** | ● | ✓ (Primary) | ✓ (Primary) |
| **Social Psychology** | ✓ (Primary) | ✓ (Primary) | ● |
| **Anthropology** | ● | ● | ✓ (Primary) |
| **Political Science** | ● | ● | ✓ (Primary) |
| **Economics** | ✓ (Primary) | ● | ✓ (Primary) |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Application: Linking Discipline Concepts to OB Variables

**Illustration 1 — Psychology → Motivation Theory**
- **Step 1:** Psychology identifies the concept of *need*.
- **Step 2:** Maslow (psychologist) builds a hierarchy of needs.
- **Step 3:** OB adopts and adapts it as the basis for designing pay, benefits, and job enrichment.
- **Result:** Manager uses Maslow to design motivation strategies at the **individual level**.

**Illustration 2 — Sociology → Group Dynamics**
- **Step 1:** Sociology identifies that individuals behave differently in groups than alone.
- **Step 2:** Sociologists (e.g., Elton Mayo — Hawthorne studies) observe informal groups in organisations.
- **Step 3:** OB adopts this to design team-building, group decision-making processes, and group incentive systems.
- **Result:** Manager understands and harnesses **group behaviour**.

**Illustration 3 — Anthropology → Organisational Culture**
- **Step 1:** Anthropology recognises that every human group develops shared values, rituals, and norms.
- **Step 2:** Anthropologists (e.g., Edward T. Hall, Geert Hofstede) study cultures in organisations.
- **Step 3:** OB uses this to identify how organisational culture influences employee behaviour.
- **Result:** Manager designs culture-change programmes and cross-cultural training.

**Illustration 4 — Political Science → Power & Conflict**
- **Step 1:** Political Science analyses how power is distributed and contested.
- **Step 2:** Concepts such as authority, influence, and negotiation are studied.
- **Step 3:** OB uses these to understand workplace politics, conflict resolution, and negotiation tactics.
- **Result:** Manager effectively manages **organisational politics and conflict**.

**Illustration 5 — Social Psychology → Attitude & Communication**
- **Step 1:** Social Psychology studies how individuals influence each other.
- **Step 2:** Concepts like conformity, persuasion, groupthink are developed.
- **Step 3:** OB uses these to design persuasive communication, training, and team interventions.
- **Result:** Manager improves **interpersonal and team communication**.

**Illustration 6 — Economics → Decision-Making & Rewards**
- **Step 1:** Economics studies decision-making under scarcity and rational choice.
- **Step 2:** Theories of compensation, agency, and behavioural economics emerge.
- **Step 3:** OB uses these to design reward systems and rational HR decisions.
- **Result:** Manager designs **efficient reward systems and rational decisions**.

### 3.4 Logical Derivation — Why "Behavioural Science" is a Collective Term

A mathematical-style abstraction of how the disciplines combine:

$$
\text{OB}_{\text{knowledge}} = \alpha P + \beta S + \gamma A + \delta \Pi + \epsilon E + \zeta \Sigma
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Psychological contributions
- $S$ = Sociological contributions
- $A$ = Anthropological contributions
- $\Pi$ = Political Science contributions
- $E$ = Economic contributions
- $\Sigma$ = Social Psychological contributions
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta, \epsilon, \zeta$ = relative weights of each contribution depending on the OB problem being studied.

> [!NOTE]
> For exam purposes, the symbolic equation is **not** required to be written. However, understanding the idea that OB is a **weighted, integrated synthesis** is critical for higher-order answers.

### 3.5 Summary of the Step-by-Step Analytical Reasoning

| Step | Reasoning | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Recognise the multi-dimensional nature of workplace behaviour | Define the problem |
| 2 | Identify that one discipline is insufficient | Need for interdisciplinary approach |
| 3 | Borrow concepts from each discipline | Build the OB vocabulary |
| 4 | Integrate the concepts at three levels of analysis | Form the OB framework |
| 5 | Apply scientific methods to test and refine | Build evidence-based OB |
| 6 | Translate insights into managerial action | Practical utility |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
