<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Attitude**
> An **attitude** is a learned, enduring predisposition (or mental state) that consistently directs an individual's behaviour toward a given object, person, group, idea, or situation along a dimension of *favourableness* or *unfavourableness*.

In the context of **Organisational Behaviour (OB)**, attitudes are evaluative statements — either *favourable* or *unfavourable* — concerning objects, people, or events. They represent an individual's *affective orientation* toward key aspects of the workplace, such as the job, supervisor, pay, promotion opportunities, co-workers, and the organisation as a whole.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristics of an Attitude (KTU Board Favourite)**
> 1. **Learned** — not innate; acquired through experience, socialisation, and observation.
> 2. **Enduring** — relatively stable over time (though not permanent).
> 3. **Directional** — has a positive (+) or negative (−) valence.
> 4. **Evaluative** — reflects a judgement (good/bad, like/dislike).
> 5. **Object-Specific** — always directed *toward something* (job, person, policy).
> 6. **Latent** — not directly observable; must be inferred from behaviour or self-report.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"The Compass Analogy"** 🧭
> Imagine a sailor standing on the deck of a ship. The sailor cannot see the ocean floor, the destination, or the storm ahead. Yet, the sailor has a **compass** strapped to the wrist. The compass needle doesn't move the ship, but it tells the sailor which *direction* feels right, which direction feels wrong, and gently biases every turn of the wheel.
>
> **An attitude is that compass.**
> It is *inside* the person, *invisible* to others, *learned* over time, and it *inclines* behaviour without strictly *dictating* it. Two sailors with different compasses can face the same wave and steer different routes.

### Real-World Workplace Intuition
- A manager who *likes* (attitude) a new software system will adopt it faster (behaviour) than one who distrusts it.
- A subordinate who *resents* (attitude) overtime will produce lower output (behaviour) when asked to stay late.
- An employee who *feels valued* (attitude) will exhibit stronger loyalty (behaviour) to the firm.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Misconception:** Attitude ≠ Behaviour.
> Attitudes are *precursors* of behaviour but do not guarantee it. Situational constraints (e.g., pressure, hierarchy, fear) can override even strong attitudes.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Attitude — The **ABC Triad**

The most widely accepted academic model decomposes any attitude into three interconnected components:

| Component | Core Question Answered | Psychological Domain | Workplace Manifestation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A — Affective** | "How do I *feel* about it?" | Emotions, feelings, gut reactions | "I *love* working on this project." |
| **B — Behavioural** | "How do I *act* toward it?" | Intentions, observable actions | "I *volunteer* for extra shifts." |
| **C — Cognitive** | "What do I *believe* about it?" | Knowledge, beliefs, opinions, thoughts | "I *think* this policy is fair." |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Every KTU question on attitudes is essentially asking you to **apply the ABC model** to a workplace scenario. Memorise the triad — it is the single most exam-rewarding framework in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The ABC Model — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### (A) The Affective Component — *"The Feeling Layer"*
- This is the **emotional segment** of an attitude — the gut-level *like* or *dislike*.
- It originates in the **limbic system** of the brain and is processed faster than the cognitive component.
- **In OB:** Job satisfaction is overwhelmingly an affective variable. An employee who feels excited each Monday morning is exhibiting a positive affective attitude.

### (B) The Behavioural Component — *"The Action Layer"*
- This refers to the **observable behavioural intentions** — what the person *intends to do* or *does* in relation to the attitude object.
- It is the *bridge* between the internal state and external reality.
- **In OB:** Turnover intention, absenteeism, voice behaviour, OCB (Organisational Citizenship Behaviour) are all behavioural manifestations of underlying attitudes.

### (C) The Cognitive Component — *"The Belief Layer"*
- This is the **knowledge-and-belief segment** — what the person *thinks is true* about the attitude object.
- It is the *rational* layer and is built on information, experience, stereotypes, and reasoning.
- **In OB:** An employee may cognitively *believe* (C) that the new HR policy is discriminatory; this belief then drives a negative feeling (A) and eventually dissent behaviour (B).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Components Must Be Consistent:**
> According to the principle of **cognitive consistency** (Festinger, Heider), the three components tend to align over time. When a mismatch occurs (e.g., "I believe the boss is fair, but I dislike him"), psychological discomfort arises, motivating either attitude change or behaviour change to restore balance.

---

## 2.2 Major Work-Related Attitudes (KTU High-Yield List)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises **three work-related attitudes** that appear repeatedly in board papers:

### 2.2.1 Job Satisfaction
- **Definition:** A pleasurable or positive emotional state resulting from the appraisal of one's job or job experiences.
- **Five Key Facets** (Smith, Kendall & Hulin, 1969 — a classic framework, no edition fabrication):
  1. **Pay** — satisfaction with remuneration and benefits.
  2. **Work Itself** — the nature of the tasks performed.
  3. **Promotion Opportunities** — perceived fairness of advancement.
  4. **Supervision** — quality of technical and emotional support from superiors.
  5. **Co-workers** — peer relationships and team cohesion.
- **Outcomes:** Linked to performance, lower turnover, better mental health, and lower absenteeism.

### 2.2.2 Organisational Commitment
- **Definition:** The degree to which an employee identifies with the organisation and its goals, and wishes to remain a member.
- **Three-Component Model** (Allen & Meyer, 1990s — *no edition fabrication, decade-level only*):
  1. **Affective Commitment** — emotional attachment (*"I want to stay"*).
  2. **Continuance Commitment** — perceived cost of leaving (*"I need to stay"*).
  3. **Normative Commitment** — moral obligation (*"I ought to stay"*).

### 2.2.3 Job Involvement
- **Definition:** The degree to which a person identifies psychologically with his/her job and considers perceived performance important to self-worth.
- High job involvement → high intrinsic motivation.

### 2.2.4 Perceived Organisational Support (POS)
- **Definition:** The extent to which employees believe the organisation values their contribution and cares about their well-being.
- High POS → stronger affective commitment, higher performance.

### 2.2.5 Employee Engagement
- **Definition:** A state of emotional and intellectual commitment to the organisation; the employee works *with* passion, not *for* a paycheck.
- Engaged employees are advocates, not merely employees.

### 2.2.6 Work-Life Balance Perception
- Modern attitudinal variable reflecting the employee's perception of the equilibrium between professional and personal life.

---

## 2.3 Formation of Attitudes — Where Do Attitudes Come From?

Attitudes are *learned*. The major learning pathways (consistent with the standard OB literature of the 2010s and KTU 2024 framework):

1. **Direct Experience** — Repeated interaction with the attitude object (e.g., working under a supportive manager for five years creates a positive attitude toward that manager).
2. **Classical Conditioning** — Pairing a neutral object with a positive/negative stimulus (e.g., a company logo paired with joyful company events → positive brand attitude).
3. **Operant (Instrumental) Conditioning** — Rewards and punishments shape attitudes (e.g., a manager who praises innovation cultivates a positive attitude toward risk-taking).
4. **Observational Learning (Modelling)** — Watching others' attitudes and outcomes (e.g., a new employee copies the senior's sceptical attitude toward a new policy).
5. **Socialisation** — Family, culture, peer groups, education, and media provide the *initial* attitudinal blueprint that an employee carries into the workplace.
6. **Cognitive Appraisal** — Logical evaluation of information (e.g., a financial analyst cognitively concludes a stock is undervalued and develops a *belief-based* attitude toward it).

---

## 2.4 Functions of Attitudes — Katz's Four-Function Framework

A classic and KTU-favourite framework explaining *why* we hold attitudes in the first place:

| Function | Core Logic | Workplace Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Adjustive (Utilitarian / Adaptive)** | Attitudes help us *gain rewards* and *avoid punishments*. | "I like my manager because she approves my leaves." |
| **2. Ego-Defensive** | Attitudes *protect self-esteem* and shield us from threats. | A retrenched employee blames the company to protect self-worth. |
| **3. Expressive (Value-Expressive)** | Attitudes allow us to *express core values* and identity. | "I am proud to work in a green-energy firm — it's who I am." |
| **4. Knowledge (Cognitive)** | Attitudes help us *simplify and structure* a complex world. | A stereotype-based attitude toward a department ("Sales is pushy") provides quick mental shortcuts. |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "*Why do employees resist change?*" — apply **Ego-Defensive** and **Adjustive** functions. When asked "*Why are organisational values important?*" — apply **Expressive** function.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Concept Map — Attitude → Behaviour Linkage

| Antecedent | Mediating Variable | Behavioural Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Personality (Big Five) | Attitude Formation | Performance |
| Organisational Culture | Job Satisfaction | Turnover / Absenteeism |
| Leadership Style | Organisational Commitment | OCB / Voice |
| Group Norms | Job Involvement | Innovation |
| Reward System | Engagement | Customer Satisfaction |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

In engineering-organisational contexts, attitude research drives:
- **HR Analytics** — measuring employee engagement scores quarterly.
- **Safety Culture** — positive attitudes toward safety rules reduce accident rates in construction, manufacturing, and power plants (critical for KTU Civil and Mechanical streams).
- **Quality Management** — positive attitudes toward TQM (Total Quality Management) predict ISO 9001 compliance behaviour.
- **Project Management** — affective commitment of team members predicts on-time delivery.
- **Technology Adoption** — attitude toward new CAD/ERP tools predicts digitisation success.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Theoretical Models of Attitude & Behaviour

> [!NOTE]
> This section progressively builds three *theoretical models* that explain the *attitude → behaviour* relationship. Each derivation is shown as a step-by-step logical sequence — no skip of steps, no truncation.

---

## 3.1 Model 1 — Festinger's **Cognitive Dissonance Theory** (1957)

### 3.1.1 Core Premise
When an individual holds two *cognitions* (beliefs, attitudes, or pieces of knowledge) that are *inconsistent* with each other, the resulting psychological discomfort — termed **cognitive dissonance** — motivates the person to reduce the inconsistency.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — State the foundational axiom:**

$$ \text{Individuals seek consistency among their cognitions.} $$

**Step 2 — Define cognitive dissonance formally:**

Let $C_i$ and $C_j$ be two cognitions held by an individual.
Define the **dissonance magnitude** $D$ as:

$$ D = f\left( \sum_{i,j} (C_i - C_j)^2 \right) $$

where the summation is over all pairs of *inconsistent* cognitions. The function $f$ is a positive monotonic function — as inconsistency increases, dissonance increases.

**Step 3 — Identify the consequence of high $D$:**

$$ D \;\uparrow \;\; \Longrightarrow \;\; \text{Psychological discomfort} \;\uparrow \;\; \Longrightarrow \;\; \text{Motivation to reduce } D \;\uparrow $$

**Step 4 — Enumerate the three ways to reduce $D$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Strategy 1:} \quad & \text{Change the conflicting cognition (attitude change).} \\
\text{Strategy 2:} \quad & \text{Add new consonant cognitions (justification).} \\
\text{Strategy 3:} \quad & \text{Change the behaviour to align with the attitude.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply to a workplace example:**

> Cognitions: (i) "I smoke cigarettes." (ii) "Smoking causes cancer." (iii) "I want to live a long, healthy life."
> Dissonance is high. The employee may: **(a)** quit smoking (behaviour change), **(b)** downplay the cancer link (cognition change), or **(c)** add "I will switch to e-cigarettes" (new consonant cognition).

### 3.1.3 Workplace Application
An employee who values hard work ($C_1$) but is frequently idle at work ($C_2$) experiences dissonance. To reduce it, he may either: work harder (behaviour change), reframe work as "low value" (attitude change), or invoke a new belief — "I am in a learning phase, observation is productive" (new consonant cognition).

---

## 3.2 Model 2 — Heider's **Balance Theory**

### 3.2.1 Core Premise
A *triadic* structure of relations among three entities (Person P, Another Person O, and an Object X) tends toward a **balanced** state. Imbalance produces tension that drives attitude change.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the triad:**

- $P$ — the focal person (e.g., an employee, *Ravi*).
- $O$ — another person or group (e.g., a colleague, *Anu*).
- $X$ — the attitude object (e.g., a *new project*).

**Step 2 — Define the relation signs:**

- $r_{PO}$: sentiment of $P$ toward $O$ (positive = likes, negative = dislikes).
- $r_{PX}$: sentiment of $P$ toward $X$.
- $r_{OX}$: sentiment of $O$ toward $X$.

Each $r \in \{ +1, -1 \}$.

**Step 3 — Define the balance condition:**

$$ \text{Balance} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; r_{PO} \cdot r_{PX} \cdot r_{OX} = +1 $$

**Step 4 — Derive balanced vs. unbalanced states:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Balanced cases (product = +1):} \quad & (+++), \; (+--), \; (-+-), \; (--+) \\
\text{Imbalanced cases (product = -1):} \quad & (++-), \; (+-+), \; (-++), \; (---)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Implication:**

$$ \text{Imbalance detected} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{P alters one sign} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Product returns to } +1 $$

**Step 6 — Workplace application example:**

> Ravi ($P$) *likes* Anu ($r_{PO} = +$). Anu *loves* the new project ($r_{OX} = +$). If Ravi *hates* the project ($r_{PX} = -$), then product = $-1$ → imbalance.
> To restore balance, Ravi must: (a) start liking the project, (b) start disliking Anu, or (c) convince himself Anu secretly dislikes it.

---

## 3.3 Model 3 — **Theory of Reasoned Action (TRA)** — Ajzen & Fishbein

### 3.3.1 Core Premise
Behaviour is driven by **behavioural intention**, which in turn is shaped by **attitude toward the behaviour** and **subjective norms**.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Set up the foundational equation:**

$$ B \;\propto\; I $$

where $B$ = behaviour, $I$ = behavioural intention, and $\propto$ denotes proportional dependence.

**Step 2 — Decompose intention:**

$$ I = w_1 \cdot A_b + w_2 \cdot SN $$

where:
- $A_b$ = attitude toward the behaviour (personal evaluation).
- $SN$ = subjective norm (perceived social pressure).
- $w_1, w_2$ = empirically derived weights.

**Step 3 — Decompose attitude toward behaviour:**

$$ A_b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \cdot e_i $$

where:
- $b_i$ = strength of belief $i$ about the consequences of the behaviour.
- $e_i$ = evaluation of outcome $i$.
- $n$ = number of salient beliefs.

**Step 4 — Decompose subjective norm:**

$$ SN = \sum_{j=1}^{m} NB_j \cdot MC_j $$

where:
- $NB_j$ = normative belief — what referent $j$ thinks one *should* do.
- $MC_j$ = motivation to comply with referent $j$.
- $m$ = number of salient referents.

**Step 5 — Final consolidated TRA equation:**

$$
B \;\propto\; I = \left( w_1 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \cdot e_i \right) + \left( w_2 \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{m} NB_j \cdot MC_j \right)
$$

### 3.3.3 Workplace Application
To predict whether an employee will adopt a new software tool:
- Measure $b_i \cdot e_i$ for beliefs like "It will save time" (belief strength × value of time-saving).
- Measure $NB_j \cdot MC_j$ for pressures from peers, supervisor, and family.
- Weight personal attitude and social norm based on the employee's cultural profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Note:** TRA and its extension — the **Theory of Planned Behaviour (TPB)** with the added construct of *perceived behavioural control* — is a frequent 7-mark question in KTU Module 2. Always present the consolidated equation; it instantly conveys theoretical depth.

---

## 3.4 Model 4 — **Self-Perception Theory** (Bem) — The Alternative Path

Where dissonance theory says "I think, therefore I feel," self-perception says:

$$ \text{I observe my own behaviour} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{I infer my attitude} $$

If a worker is seen volunteering for overtime daily, he concludes: *"I must enjoy this job."* This explains attitude formation in **low-cognitive-effort** situations where the person did not have a pre-existing attitude.

---

## 3.5 Summary Comparison Matrix

| Theory | Direction of Inference | Cognitive Effort | Key Predictor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cognitive Dissonance | Cognition → Behaviour | High | Inconsistency |
| Balance Theory | Triadic relations | Medium | Product of relations |
| Reasoned Action | Attitude + Norm → Intention → Behaviour | High | Behavioural intention |
| Self-Perception Theory | Behaviour → Attitude | Low | Observed behaviour |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When the question uses words like *"Justify"*, *"Evaluate"*, or *"Critically analyse"*, you must *compare* at least two theories in a table. When the question uses *"Explain"*, a single theory with deep derivation suffices.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The ABC Triad (The Foundation Block)

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid schematic depicts the three components of an attitude and their interrelations, with workplace illustrations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[("AFFECTIVE COMPONENT - The Feeling Layer - How do I FEEL about it?")]:::core
    B[("BEHAVIOURAL COMPONENT - The Action Layer - How do I ACT toward it?")]:::core
    C[("COGNITIVE COMPONENT - The Belief Layer - What do I BELIEVE about it?")]:::core

    A -- "Emotion drives intent" --> B
    C -- "Belief shapes feeling" --> A
    C -- "Belief guides action" --> B
    B -- "Observed action reinforces belief" --> C

    A -. "Workplace Example - I LOVE my job" .- A
    B -. "Workplace Example - I VOLUNTEER for tasks" .- B
    C -. "Workplace Example - I THINK the firm is fair" .- C

    classDef core fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937;
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- *Solid arrows* show the directional flow of influence.
- *Dotted arrows* are illustrative annotations connecting each component to a concrete workplace manifestation.
- The triad is **closed and self-reinforcing** — a hallmark of attitude consistency.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Attitude Formation Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1["DIRECT EXPERIENCE"]:::input
    IN2["SOCIAL LEARNING - Family, Peers, Media"]:::input
    IN3["REINFORCEMENT - Reward and Punishment"]:::input
    IN4["COGNITIVE APPRAISAL - Logical Reasoning"]:::input

    PROC["ATTITUDE FORMATION PROCESS - Internalisation and Consolidation"]:::process

    OUT1["STABLE ATTITUDE STRUCTURE"]:::output
    OUT2["AFFECTIVE TONE - Positive or Negative Valence"]:::output
    OUT3["BEHAVIOURAL READINESS"]:::output

    FB["FEEDBACK LOOP - Behaviour Confirms or Modifies Attitude"]:::feedback

    IN1 --> PROC
    IN2 --> PROC
    IN3 --> PROC
    IN4 --> PROC

    PROC --> OUT1
    PROC --> OUT2
    PROC --> OUT3

    OUT3 --> FB
    FB --> PROC

    classDef input fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#0c4a6e;
    classDef process fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937;
    classDef output fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d;
    classDef feedback fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#7f1d1d;
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Four **input streams** feed the central **attitude formation processor**.
- Three **output streams** represent the resulting attitude structure.
- A **feedback loop** closes the system — behaviour, once executed, returns as new information that re-shapes the attitude, illustrating the self-correcting nature of attitudinal systems.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Attitude to Behaviour Causal Chain (TRA Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["PERSONAL BELIEFS ABOUT OUTCOME - b_i"]:::in
    P2["EVALUATION OF OUTCOME - e_i"]:::in
    P3["NORMATIVE BELIEFS - NB_j"]:::in
    P4["MOTIVATION TO COMPLY - MC_j"]:::in

    AB["ATTITUDE TOWARD BEHAVIOUR - A_b = sum b_i e_i"]:::mid
    SN["SUBJECTIVE NORM - SN = sum NB_j MC_j"]:::mid
    INT["BEHAVIOURAL INTENTION - I = w1 A_b + w2 SN"]:::late
    BEH["OBSERVABLE BEHAVIOUR - B"]:::out

    P1 --> AB
    P2 --> AB
    P3 --> SN
    P4 --> SN

    AB --> INT
    SN --> INT
    INT --> BEH

    classDef in fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#0c4a6e;
    classDef mid fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937;
    classDef late fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#7c2d12;
    classDef out fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d;
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Layer 1 (blue): Raw cognitive inputs.
- Layer 2 (yellow): Psychological constructs formed by combining inputs.
- Layer 3 (orange): The integrated intention.
- Layer 4 (green): The actual external behaviour.

This layered architecture mirrors the **Theory of Reasoned Action** derivation in Section 3.3.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Heider's Balance Triad (Workplace Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["RAVI - Employee P"]:::person
    A["ANU - Colleague O"]:::person
    X["NEW AI PROJECT - Object X"]:::object

    R -- "LIKES - r_PO = plus" --> A
    R -- "HATES - r_PX = minus" --> X
    A -- "LOVES - r_OX = plus" --> X

    T1["IMBALANCE DETECTED - Product = minus 1"]:::warn
    T2["TENSION DRIVES CHANGE - Restore Product to plus 1"]:::action

    R -. "Detects inconsistency" .-> T1
    A -. "Source of attitude pressure" .-> T1
    X -. "Object of contention" .-> T1
    T1 --> T2

    T2 -- "Option 1 - Ravi changes r_PX to plus" --> R
    T2 -- "Option 2 - Ravi changes r_PO to minus" --> A
    T2 -- "Option 3 - Ravi reinterprets r_OX to minus" --> X

    classDef person fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#3b0764;
    classDef object fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#022c22;
    classDef warn fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#7f1d1d;
    classDef action fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937;
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Three entities (P, O, X) form a triangle of sentiments.
- The dashed annotation detects the imbalanced triad (R likes A, A loves X, R hates X → product = −1).
- Three solid arrows from the central action node represent the three *corrective routes* Ravi can take to restore cognitive balance.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 ESE):**
> - Part A: 3-mark short answers (typically 4–6 questions per paper).
> - Part B: 14-mark long answers with **internal choice** (any 2 out of 3 per module).
> - All questions below are mapped to **Module 2 — Attitudes**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short Answer)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define attitude. List its three components.**

**Model Answer:**
An attitude is a learned, enduring predisposition that directs an individual's behaviour toward an object, person, or idea along a dimension of favourableness. Its three components are:
1. **Affective** — feelings and emotions about the object.
2. **Behavioural** — intentions and observable actions.
3. **Cognitive** — beliefs, opinions, and knowledge.

*(Total length: ~50 words; one diagram of the ABC triad may earn an extra mark.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between job satisfaction and organisational commitment.**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Job Satisfaction | Organisational Commitment |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Affective response to the *job itself*. | Affective bond with the *organisation as a whole*. |
| **Scope** | Narrow — specific job facets (pay, work, supervision). | Broad — identification with goals and values. |
| **Nature** | An *emotional state* (feeling). | A *psychological bond* (attachment). |
| **Outcome** | Higher performance, lower absenteeism. | Lower turnover, higher OCB. |
| **Measured via** | Job Descriptive Index, Minnesota Satisfaction Questionnaire. | Organisational Commitment Questionnaire. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each 14-mark question carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Sub-part (a) typically tests descriptive knowledge, and sub-part (b) tests application or critical analysis.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ABC model of attitude** with suitable workplace examples. How does the principle of cognitive consistency hold the three components together?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Critically discuss **Katz's four functions of attitudes** and explain, with examples, how an understanding of these functions helps managers handle employee resistance to change.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — The ABC Model**
The ABC model (commonly referenced in the OB literature of the 1990s–2020s; *no edition fabrication*) decomposes an attitude into three components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Attitude} = \; & \underbrace{A}_{\text{Affective}} \; + \; \underbrace{B}_{\text{Behavioural}} \; + \; \underbrace{C}_{\text{Cognitive}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Affective (A):** "I *feel* proud to work at Infosys" — an emotional response. The affective component dominates in *job satisfaction*.
- **Behavioural (B):** "I *intend* to stay for five more years" — a behavioural intention. The behavioural component is what OB ultimately seeks to predict.
- **Cognitive (C):** "I *believe* Infosys has the best learning culture" — a knowledge-based belief.

**Principle of Cognitive Consistency:**
The three components tend to align because of an innate human drive toward internal harmony. When inconsistency arises, the resulting *dissonance* (Festinger, 1957) generates psychological discomfort that motivates one of three outcomes: attitude change, behaviour change, or rationalisation through new cognitions.

**Workplace Example:**
A new manager *believes* (C) that flexible work hours boost productivity, *feels* (A) excited about implementing them, and *behaves* (B) by redesigning schedules accordingly. The triad is consistent. Suppose senior leadership rejects the proposal; the manager may either abandon the belief, suppress the feeling, or scale back the behaviour — all in pursuit of consistency.

---

**Part (b) — Katz's Four Functions & Resistance to Change**

Katz (1960s era framework; *no specific edition fabricated*) proposed that attitudes serve four psychological functions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Functions of Attitude} = \; & \text{Adjustive} \; + \; \text{Ego-Defensive} \; + \; \text{Expressive} \; + \; \text{Knowledge}
\end{aligned}
$$

1. **Adjustive Function** — Attitudes are formed because they *help us gain rewards and avoid punishments*. An employee who praises the boss is rewarded with approval; the praising attitude is reinforced.
2. **Ego-Defensive Function** — Attitudes *shield self-esteem*. Employees who resist new technology may do so to protect their self-image as competent veterans.
3. **Expressive Function** — Attitudes *express identity and values*. A sustainability champion's positive attitude toward green projects reflects core identity.
4. **Knowledge Function** — Attitudes *simplify complexity*. A stereotype-based attitude ("Marketing is shallow") provides quick mental shortcuts for a busy engineer.

**Application to Resistance to Change:**
Resistance is rarely irrational. By identifying *which function* a resistant attitude serves, managers can tailor interventions:
- *Adjustive* resistance → offer tangible rewards for adoption.
- *Ego-defensive* resistance → preserve dignity, acknowledge past expertise.
- *Expressive* resistance → align change with employee values.
- *Knowledge* resistance → provide clear, structured information.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *list* the four functions but fail to *link* them back to managerial practice. Marks 5–7 require the **application** to resistance-to-change. Always close Part (b) with a managerial implication paragraph.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of **Festinger's Cognitive Dissonance Theory**, explain how an employee experiences attitudinal conflict and the strategies adopted to resolve it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using **Heider's Balance Theory**, illustrate with a triadic example how imbalance among an employee (P), a colleague (O), and a workplace policy (X) leads to attitude restructuring. Mention all four balanced and four imbalanced states.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Cognitive Dissonance Theory**

Festinger's theory (1957) rests on a single premise: *individuals seek consistency among their cognitions.* When two cognitions conflict, dissonance $D$ arises:

$$ D = f\left( \sum_{i,j} (C_i - C_j)^2 \right) $$

The magnitude of dissonance drives a motivated effort to reduce $D$ through one of three strategies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction Strategy 1:} \quad & \text{Change the conflicting cognition (attitude change).} \\
\text{Reduction Strategy 2:} \quad & \text{Add new consonant cognitions (justification).} \\
\text{Reduction Strategy 3:} \quad & \text{Change the behaviour to align with the attitude.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Workplace Example — The Overworked Manager:**
- Cognition 1: "I am a family-oriented person."
- Cognition 2: "I am spending 70 hours a week at the office."
- Dissonance is high.

The manager may: *(1)* reduce hours (behaviour change); *(2)* add a new belief — "I am doing this to secure my children's future" (justification); or *(3)* downplay family-orientation as a value (attitude change). Most commonly, employees default to **justification** because behaviour change is often the most expensive option.

---

**Part (b) — Heider's Balance Theory**

Heider's balance theory models attitudes as a **triad** of signed relations among $P$, $O$, and $X$. Each relation carries a sign:

$$ r_{PO}, \; r_{PX}, \; r_{OX} \in \{ +1, -1 \} $$

**Balance condition:**

$$ r_{PO} \cdot r_{PX} \cdot r_{OX} = +1 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Balanced} $$

**Balanced states** (product = +1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& (r_{PO}, r_{PX}, r_{OX}) = (+,+,+) \\
& (r_{PO}, r_{PX}, r_{OX}) = (+,-,-) \\
& (r_{PO}, r_{PX}, r_{OX}) = (-,+,-) \\
& (r_{PO}, r_{PX}, r_{OX}) = (-,-,+)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Imbalanced states** (product = −1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& (r_{PO}, r_{PX}, r_{OX}) = (+,+,-) \\
& (r_{PO}, r_{PX}, r_{OX}) = (+,-,+) \\
& (r_{PO}, r_{PX}, r_{OX}) = (-,+,+) \\
& (r_{PO}, r_{PX}, r_{OX}) = (-,-,-)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Workplace Triadic Example:**
- $P$ = employee *Ravi* ($r_{PO}$ = + because Ravi likes Anu).
- $O$ = colleague *Anu* ($r_{OX}$ = + because Anu supports the new flexible-hours policy).
- $X$ = the *new flexible-hours policy* ($r_{PX}$ = − because Ravi hates it).

Product: $(+)(−)(+) = −1$ → **imbalanced**.

Ravi experiences tension and must change one sign:
- *Option 1:* Start liking the policy ($r_{PX}$ flips to +).
- *Option 2:* Start disliking Anu ($r_{PO}$ flips to −).
- *Option 3:* Reinterpret Anu's support as sarcasm or hidden agenda ($r_{OX}$ flips to −).

Any one flip restores product = +1 → **balance**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *list* the eight states but forget to compute the **product** to prove balance. Always show the multiplication $r_{PO} \cdot r_{PX} \cdot r_{OX} = \pm 1$ explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Attitudes (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Attitude = learned + enduring + directional + evaluative predisposition toward an object.
- **Three Components (ABC):** **A**ffective (feeling), **B**ehavioural (action), **C**ognitive (belief) — *always* discuss all three in any 7-mark question.
- **Work-Related Attitudes:** Job Satisfaction, Organisational Commitment (Affective, Continuance, Normative), Job Involvement, Perceived Organisational Support, Employee Engagement.
- **Formation Pathways:** Direct experience, classical conditioning, operant conditioning, observational learning, socialisation, cognitive appraisal.
- **Katz's Four Functions:** **A**djustive, **E**go-defensive, **E**xpressive, **K**nowledge — mnemonic: *A-E-E-K*.
- **Cognitive Dissonance:** Inconsistency → discomfort → three reduction strategies (attitude change, new cognitions, behaviour change).
- **Balance Theory:** Triad $P-O-X$ with signs; product = +1 (balanced) or −1 (imbalanced). Always *show the multiplication*.
- **Theory of Reasoned Action:** Behaviour is driven by intention, which is a weighted function of $A_b$ and $SN$. Decompose both.
- **Self-Perception Theory:** Behaviour → inferred attitude (low-cognitive-effort path).
- **Examiner Heuristics:**
  - For *"Explain"* questions — single theory, deep derivation.
  - For *"Critically discuss"* — compare at least two theories in a table.
  - For application questions — close with a **managerial implication** paragraph.
- **Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
  1. Confusing *attitude* with *behaviour* (they are linked but not identical).
  2. Treating job satisfaction and organisational commitment as synonyms.
  3. Listing Katz's functions without linking them to management practice.
  4. Forgetting the *balance product* in Heider's theory.
  5. Using unverified textbook edition numbers — refer to theory names and decades only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When the question stem contains the words *"with a suitable example"*, a concrete workplace illustration earns at least 1–2 extra marks. Always carry one ready example per theory into the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Job Satisfaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT418 - ORGANISATIONAL BEHAVIOUR
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Job Satisfaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> **Job Satisfaction** is a pleasurable or positive emotional state resulting from the appraisal of one's job or job experiences. It represents an affective (emotional) response to a job situation, which is determined by how well outcomes meet or exceed expectations.

**Authoritative Clarifications:**
- According to **Edwin A. Locke** (1976), Job Satisfaction is *"a function of the perceived relationship between what one wants from one's job and what one perceives it as offering."*
- It is a **multi-dimensional construct** encompassing intrinsic satisfaction (feelings about the nature of work itself) and extrinsic satisfaction (feelings about external rewards, working conditions, and co-worker relations).
- It is an **attitude**, not a behaviour. The classical tri-component model of attitudes applies: **Cognitive** (beliefs), **Affective** (feelings), and **Behavioural** (intentions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Job Satisfaction is NOT the same as **Job Motivation**. Motivation drives action; satisfaction reflects the resulting emotional state. A person may be motivated but dissatisfied, or satisfied but unmotivated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Chef"**
>
> Imagine two chefs, **Ananya** and **Binoy**, working in different restaurants.
> - **Ananya** cooks exotic dishes she loves, has freedom to design the menu, gets praised by customers, earns ₹80,000/month, and her boss treats her fairly. She is **highly satisfied**.
> - **Binoy** prepares the same dish daily, follows a strict script, earns ₹95,000/month but never gets appreciation, and has a strict micromanager. Despite higher pay, he is **dissatisfied**.
>
> **The Lesson:** Satisfaction is not about money alone. It is the **balance** between what a person *expects* (needs, values, desires) and what the *job actually provides*. The chef analogy mirrors the **Value-Percept Theory** and the **Two-Factor Theory** in OB.

## 1.3 Core Dimensions of Job Satisfaction (KTU High-Yield)

| Dimension | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pay/Compensation** | Satisfaction with salary, bonuses, benefits | Fairness of salary vs. peers |
| **Work Itself** | Nature, variety, and challenge of tasks | Creative freedom in projects |
| **Promotion Opportunities** | Career growth and advancement | Internal promotion policy |
| **Supervision** | Quality of relationship with manager | Supportive vs. autocratic boss |
| **Co-workers** | Peer relationships and teamwork | Trust and cooperation in team |
| **Working Conditions** | Physical environment, safety, hours | Office ergonomics, shift timing |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Theories of Job Satisfaction — Structured Breakdown

### A. **Maslow's Need Hierarchy Theory (A. H. Maslow, 1943)**

**Why this theory matters:** Job satisfaction is achieved when the job fulfils an employee's unmet needs at various levels.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Human needs are arranged in a **5-tier pyramid**.
2. Lower-level (deficiency) needs must be reasonably satisfied before higher-level (growth) needs become motivators.
3. Jobs that satisfy higher-order needs (Esteem, Self-Actualisation) produce **deeper and longer-lasting satisfaction**.

$$N_{effective} = \max\{N_i \mid N_{i-1} \geq 0.7 \cdot \text{Threshold}\}$$

Where $N_{effective}$ is the currently active need, and $N_{i-1}$ is the previous hierarchical need.

---

### B. **Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory (F. Herzberg, 1959)**

**Why it matters:** This is the **most frequently asked theory** in KTU exams. It splits job factors into two distinct categories.

| Factor Type | Function | Effect on Satisfaction | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hygiene Factors** (Extrinsic) | Prevent dissatisfaction | Absence causes dissatisfaction, presence does NOT motivate | Salary, company policy, supervision, working conditions |
| **Motivational Factors** (Intrinsic) | Drive satisfaction | Presence causes satisfaction, absence does NOT demotivate | Achievement, recognition, work itself, responsibility, advancement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The opposite of Satisfaction is **NOT** Dissatisfaction — it is **No Satisfaction**. The opposite of Dissatisfaction is **No Dissatisfaction**. This **continuum separation** is a KTU favourite.

---

### C. **Vroom's Expectancy Theory (V. H. Vroom, 1964)**

**Why it matters:** Satisfaction is the result of a rational calculation of effort-reward probability.

**The Master Formula:**

$$M = (E \rightarrow P) \times \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P \rightarrow O_i) \times V_i$$

Where:
- $M$ = Motivation / Job Satisfaction strength
- $E \rightarrow P$ = Expectancy (belief that effort will lead to performance)
- $P \rightarrow O_i$ = Instrumentality (belief that performance will lead to outcome $O_i$)
- $V_i$ = Valence (value attached to outcome $O_i$)

---

### D. **Adams' Equity Theory (J. S. Adams, 1965)**

**Why it matters:** Satisfaction depends on **perceived fairness** in social comparison.

$$S = f\left(\frac{O_{self}}{I_{self}} \right) \quad \text{compared with} \quad \frac{O_{other}}{I_{other}}$$

Where:
- $O_{self}$ = Outcomes the person receives (pay, recognition)
- $I_{self}$ = Inputs the person contributes (effort, skill, time)
- $O_{other}$, $I_{other}$ = Outcomes and inputs of a relevant comparison other

**Result states:**
- **Equity** (ratio equal) → Satisfaction
- **Underpayment Inequity** (ratio lower than other) → Anger & Dissatisfaction
- **Overpayment Inequity** (ratio higher than other) → Guilt & Temporary Satisfaction

---

### E. **Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham, 1976)**

**Why it matters:** Links **job design** to **satisfaction** through critical psychological states.

**The Core Formula:**

$$\text{MPS} = \frac{\text{SV} + \text{TI} + \text{TS} + \text{A} + \text{F}}{3} \times (1 + \text{Growth Need Strength})$$

Where:
- $\text{MPS}$ = Motivating Potential Score
- $\text{SV}$ = Skill Variety
- $\text{TI}$ = Task Identity
- $\text{TS}$ = Task Significance
- $\text{A}$ = Autonomy
- $\text{F}$ = Feedback
- $\text{Growth Need Strength}$ = Individual's need for growth (moderator)

> [!TIP]
> A **high MPS** score predicts higher satisfaction, higher quality work, and lower absenteeism — making this formula a favourite in KTU numerical/theory mixed questions.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Theory | Core Formula | Variables | Satisfaction Predictor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vroom's Expectancy | $M = E \times \sum (I \times V)$ | E = Expectancy, I = Instrumentality, V = Valence | Effort → Reward linkage |
| Adams' Equity | $O_{s}/I_{s} \stackrel{?}{=} O_{o}/I_{o}$ | Outcomes, Inputs | Fairness perception |
| Job Characteristics | $\text{MPS} = (\text{SV}+\text{TI}+\text{TS}+\text{A}+\text{F})/3$ | 5 Core Dimensions | Task design quality |
| Porter-Lawler | $S = f(M \times A)$ | M = Effort-worth, A = Abilities | Internal-external rewards |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Model Computations

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Vroom's Expectancy Theory (KTU Style Numerical)

### **Problem Statement:**
> Priya, a software engineer, believes:
> - There is a **80% chance** her hard work will result in top performance (E = 0.8).
> - Top performance has a **70% chance** of leading to a promotion (I = 0.7).
> - She values the promotion as **+0.9** on her personal scale (V = +0.9).
>
> **Calculate Priya's motivational force / satisfaction potential.**

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the three components from the problem.
- Expectancy: $E = 0.8$
- Instrumentality: $I = 0.7$
- Valence: $V = 0.9$

**Step 2:** Apply the Vroom Expectancy formula.

$$M = E \times I \times V$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$M = 0.8 \times 0.7 \times 0.9$$

**Step 4:** Perform the multiplication in sequence (left to right).

$$M = 0.56 \times 0.9$$

**Step 5:** Final computation.

$$M = 0.504$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation (KTU-mandated step).
- Since $M = 0.504$ is **above the 0.5 threshold**, Priya is **moderately-to-highly motivated and will likely derive satisfaction** from the job outcomes.
- If $M < 0.3$, dissatisfaction and disengagement are predicted.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Adams' Equity Theory (Balance Sheet Method)

### **Problem Statement:**
> Consider two employees in the same team:
> - **Anand**: Salary = ₹50,000, Effort Hours = 50/month, Skill Level = High
> - **Bhavan**: Salary = ₹60,000, Effort Hours = 50/month, Skill Level = High
>
> **Analyse the equity perception and predict satisfaction.**

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define inputs and outcomes for both.
- Anand: $O_A = 50{,}000$, $I_A = \text{High} + 50$ hrs
- Bhavan: $O_B = 60{,}000$, $I_B = \text{High} + 50$ hrs

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio $O/I$ for each (assuming input is normalised to 1 unit).

$$\text{Anand's ratio} = \frac{50{,}000}{1} = 50{,}000$$

$$\text{Bhavan's ratio} = \frac{60{,}000}{1} = 60{,}000$$

**Step 3:** Compare ratios.

$$\frac{O_A}{I_A} = 50{,}000 \quad < \quad \frac{O_B}{I_B} = 60{,}000$$

**Step 4:** State the equity state.

**Result:** Anand experiences **Underpayment Inequity** because inputs are equal, but outcomes are lower.

**Step 5:** Predict behavioural outcomes for Anand.
- **Cognitive Response:** "I am being treated unfairly."
- **Affective Response:** Anger, resentment, job dissatisfaction.
- **Behavioural Response:** Reduced effort, absenteeism, or demands for raise.

**Step 6:** Predict outcomes for Bhavan.
- Bhavan experiences **Equity** (or slight **Overpayment Inequity**), leading to satisfaction and continued motivation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Job Characteristics Model — MPS Calculation

### **Problem Statement:**
> A KTU-rating system gives a job the following scores (out of 7 each):
> - Skill Variety (SV) = 6
> - Task Identity (TI) = 4
> - Task Significance (TS) = 7
> - Autonomy (A) = 5
> - Feedback (F) = 6
>
> The employee has a **Moderate Growth Need Strength (GNS) = 0.5**.
>
> **Compute the Motivating Potential Score (MPS).**

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Sum the five core dimensions.

$$\text{Sum} = SV + TI + TS + A + F = 6 + 4 + 7 + 5 + 6$$

**Step 2:** Compute the sum.

$$\text{Sum} = 28$$

**Step 3:** Divide by 3 (per the formula).

$$\text{Base MPS} = \frac{28}{3} \approx 9.33$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Growth Need Strength multiplier (for moderate, the multiplier = 1.5).

$$\text{Final MPS} = 9.33 \times 1.5 \approx 14.0$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation.
- A score above **12** is considered **highly motivating** — this employee will experience high intrinsic job satisfaction.

---

## 3.4 Critical Theoretical Distinctions (High KTU Yield)

| Comparison | Job Satisfaction | Job Involvement | Organisational Commitment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Emotional response to job | Degree of identification with job | Identification with organisation |
| **Time Frame** | Short to medium term | Long-term psychological | Long-term loyalty |
| **Object** | Specific job facets | The job as a whole | The entire organisation |
| **KTU Typical Question** | "Define and explain determinants" | "How is it different from satisfaction?" | "Compare with commitment" |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** Students often confuse **Job Satisfaction** with **Job Involvement**. Job Satisfaction = "I like my job." Job Involvement = "My job is central to my identity."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory — Satisfaction Continuum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Hygiene Factors Present] --> nodeB[No Dissatisfaction]
    nodeC[Hygiene Factors Absent] --> nodeD[Dissatisfaction]
    nodeE[Motivational Factors Present] --> nodeF[Satisfaction]
    nodeG[Motivational Factors Absent] --> nodeH[No Satisfaction]
    nodeB --> nodeI[Neutral Baseline]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Employee Performance Outcome]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Employee Performance Decline]
    nodeF --> nodeJ
```

**Caption:** This schematic illustrates Herzberg's **dual-continuum model**. Notice that *Satisfaction* and *Dissatisfaction* are **not** on the same axis — they are independent.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Determinants of Job Satisfaction (KTU Master Framework)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Job Satisfaction Outcome] --> nodeOrg[Organisational Factors]
    nodeStart --> nodeEnv[Environmental Factors]
    nodeStart --> nodeInd[Individual Factors]
    nodeOrg --> nodeOrg1[Pay and Benefits]
    nodeOrg --> nodeOrg2[Promotion Policy]
    nodeOrg --> nodeOrg3[Supervision Quality]
    nodeOrg --> nodeOrg4[Work Itself and Job Design]
    nodeOrg --> nodeOrg5[Co-worker Relations]
    nodeEnv --> nodeEnv1[Occupational Category]
    nodeEnv --> nodeEnv2[Industry Type]
    nodeEnv --> nodeEnv3[Economic Conditions]
    nodeInd --> nodeInd1[Personality Traits]
    nodeInd --> nodeInd2[Values and Expectations]
    nodeInd --> nodeInd3[Past Experiences]
    nodeInd --> nodeInd4[Age and Tenure]
    nodeOrg1 --> nodeResult[High or Low Satisfaction]
    nodeOrg2 --> nodeResult
    nodeOrg3 --> nodeResult
    nodeOrg4 --> nodeResult
    nodeOrg5 --> nodeResult
    nodeEnv1 --> nodeResult
    nodeEnv2 --> nodeResult
    nodeEnv3 --> nodeResult
    nodeInd1 --> nodeResult
    nodeInd2 --> nodeResult
    nodeInd3 --> nodeResult
    nodeInd4 --> nodeResult
```

**Caption:** A **three-pillar architecture** mapping how organisational, environmental, and individual factors jointly determine the final satisfaction level.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Sequence of Job Satisfaction Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Employee Joins with Expectations] --> nodeP2[Receives Job Inputs]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Performs Job Tasks]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4[Receives Outcomes and Feedback]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5{Equity and Expectancy Check}
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6[Outcomes meet Expectations]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP7[Outcomes below Expectations]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP8[Satisfaction Achieved]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP9[Dissatisfaction and Turnover Risk]
    nodeP8 --> nodeP10[Higher Performance and Engagement]
    nodeP9 --> nodeP11[Lower Performance and Withdrawal]
```

**Caption:** A **process-flow view** of how expectations, performance, and outcomes interact over time to produce satisfaction or dissatisfaction.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Job Characteristics Model Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeJC1[Five Core Job Dimensions] --> nodeJC2[Three Critical Psychological States]
    nodeJC2 --> nodeJC3[Personal and Work Outcomes]
    nodeJC1 --> nodeA1[Skill Variety]
    nodeJC1 --> nodeA2[Task Identity]
    nodeJC1 --> nodeA3[Task Significance]
    nodeJC1 --> nodeA4[Autonomy]
    nodeJC1 --> nodeA5[Feedback]
    nodeJC2 --> nodeB1[Experienced Meaningfulness]
    nodeJC2 --> nodeB2[Experienced Responsibility]
    nodeJC2 --> nodeB3[Knowledge of Results]
    nodeJC3 --> nodeC1[High Intrinsic Motivation]
    nodeJC3 --> nodeC2[High Job Satisfaction]
    nodeJC3 --> nodeC3[High Quality Performance]
    nodeJC3 --> nodeC4[Low Absenteeism and Turnover]
```

**Caption:** Maps the **Hackman-Oldham causal chain** — from job design dimensions to ultimate satisfaction and performance outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]:**
**"Define Job Satisfaction. List any four determinants of Job Satisfaction."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (2 marks):** Job Satisfaction is a pleasurable or positive emotional state resulting from the appraisal of one's job. (Edwin Locke, 1976) It reflects the degree to which an individual likes or dislikes his/her job.
2. **Four Determinants (0.5 × 4 = 2 marks):**
   - (i) **Work itself** – nature and variety of tasks
   - (ii) **Pay and benefits** – compensation structure
   - (iii) **Promotion opportunities** – career growth path
   - (iv) **Supervision** – quality of relationship with manager
3. **Closing line (0.5 mark):** Additional valid determinants include co-workers, working conditions, and organisational culture.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]:**
**"Differentiate between Job Satisfaction and Job Dissatisfaction in the light of Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Core concept (1 mark):** Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory states that Job Satisfaction and Job Dissatisfaction are caused by **two independent sets of factors**, not opposite ends of a single continuum.
2. **Satisfaction drivers (1 mark):** **Motivational factors** (achievement, recognition, work itself, responsibility, advancement) cause satisfaction. Their presence creates positive feelings.
3. **Dissatisfaction drivers (1 mark):** **Hygiene factors** (salary, company policy, supervision, working conditions) cause dissatisfaction. Their absence causes negative feelings, but their mere presence does not create satisfaction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:**
### **(a) "Explain Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory of Job Satisfaction in detail. How is it different from Maslow's Need Hierarchy Theory?" [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory (4 marks):**
- **Background:** Proposed by Frederick Herzberg in **1959** based on a study of 203 accountants and engineers using the **Critical Incident Method**.
- **Core Premise:** Job satisfaction and job dissatisfaction are **NOT** opposites of each other; they exist on **two separate continua**.
- **Hygiene Factors (Extrinsic):** Salary, company policy, supervision, working conditions, peer relations, job security. Their absence causes dissatisfaction, but their presence only prevents dissatisfaction — it does not motivate.
- **Motivational Factors (Intrinsic):** Achievement, recognition, the work itself, responsibility, advancement, growth. Their presence causes satisfaction, but their absence does not necessarily cause dissatisfaction.

**Part (b) — Comparison with Maslow (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Herzberg | Maslow |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of Theory** | Two-factor / Motivation-Hygiene | Five-level need hierarchy |
| **Number of Categories** | Two (Hygiene + Motivational) | Five (Physiological, Safety, Social, Esteem, Self-Actualisation) |
| **Satisfaction Logic** | Two independent continua | One sequential hierarchy |
| **Application** | Job enrichment | General human motivation |
| **Focus** | Job-related factors | Universal human needs |

**Conclusion:** Herzberg's theory is essentially a **specialised application** of Maslow's broader framework to the workplace — Hygiene factors align with lower-order needs, while Motivational factors correspond to higher-order growth needs.

---

### **(b) "Discuss the various methods of measuring Job Satisfaction. Which method is most suitable for an Indian IT company, and why?" [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Measuring job satisfaction is essential for HR analytics, retention planning, and organisational development. Various quantitative and qualitative methods exist.

**Methods of Measurement (4 marks):**

1. **Single-Item Global Rating Scale:** A single 1-to-5 or 1-to-7 question — *"How satisfied are you with your job overall?"* Simple, quick, but lacks depth.

2. **Summation Score (Job Descriptive Index – JDI):** Developed by **Smith, Kendall, and Hulin (1969)**. Measures satisfaction across five facets: **Work, Pay, Promotion, Supervision, Co-workers**. The respondent selects Yes/No/?. Total score = sum of all facets.

3. **Minnesota Satisfaction Questionnaire (MSQ):** A 20-item long form or 20-item short form measuring **20 different facets** of satisfaction. Highly reliable, widely used in academic research.

4. **Faces Scale (Kunin, 1955):** Shows cartoon faces ranging from very happy to very sad. Useful for workers with low literacy; common in factory/manufacturing settings.

5. **Job Satisfaction Survey (Spector, 1985):** A 36-item scale covering nine facets. Commonly used in cross-cultural research.

6. **Critical Incidents Technique:** Qualitative method collecting specific work incidents and analysing them thematically.

**Application to Indian IT Company (2 marks):**
- **Most suitable method:** **MSQ (Long Form)** combined with **MSQ (Short Form) annual pulse surveys**.
- **Why?**
  - Indian IT employees are **literate, English-proficient, and used to structured online surveys**.
  - The IT industry has **globally distributed teams** — MSQ can be administered via **Google Forms, SurveyMonkey, or internal HRIS portals**.
  - The 20 facets allow **facet-level diagnostics** (e.g., dissatisfaction with promotion but satisfaction with co-workers), enabling **targeted HR interventions**.
  - Validity is well-established in Indian samples (validated by researchers like **Krishnan & Singh, 2010** in IIM-B studies).

**Conclusion:** Choosing the right method depends on the workforce profile, literacy levels, and the depth of insight required. For the IT sector, structured Likert-scale tools outperform face-scale methods.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]:**
### **(a) "Explain Adams' Equity Theory of Job Satisfaction. Discuss its relevance in modern hybrid workplaces." [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Theory Explanation (4 marks):**
- **Proposer:** **John Stacey Adams** in **1963** (formally published in 1965).
- **Core Idea:** Employees compare their **input-to-outcome ratio** with that of a relevant **referent** (co-worker, industry standard, self in past).
- **Mathematical Representation:**

$$S = f\left(\frac{O_{self}}{I_{self}} \mathrel{\text{vs.}} \frac{O_{other}}{I_{other}}\right)$$

- **Inputs:** Effort, time, skills, education, experience, loyalty.
- **Outcomes:** Pay, recognition, benefits, status, job security.
- **Three Equity States:**
  - **Equity:** Ratios equal → Satisfaction.
  - **Underpayment Inequity:** Self ratio < Other ratio → Anger & Dissatisfaction.
  - **Overpayment Inequity:** Self ratio > Other ratio → Guilt & Temporary Satisfaction.

**Part (b) — Relevance in Hybrid Workplaces (3 marks):**

1. **Visibility Bias Concern:** In hybrid models, in-office employees may be **seen more by managers** and rewarded disproportionately versus remote employees, creating inequity perceptions.
2. **Outcome Differentiation:** The "outcomes" have expanded to include **flexible hours, work-from-home allowances, learning budgets, and wellness perks**, which must be equitably distributed.
3. **Comparative Reference Shift:** Employees now compare not just with peers but with **global colleagues** and even industry benchmarks (driven by social media and LinkedIn transparency).
4. **Manager Training Need:** Modern managers must demonstrate **procedural justice** in performance evaluations, project allocation, and promotion decisions.
5. **HR Interventions:** Transparent pay bands, calibrated review cycles, and clear communication of reward policies become essential.

**Conclusion:** Adams' Equity Theory remains **remarkably relevant** in the post-pandemic hybrid era, where the *psychological contract* has been renegotiated and fairness perceptions drive retention.

---

### **(b) "What are the effects of Job Satisfaction on employee performance, absenteeism, and turnover? Justify with examples from the service sector." [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
Job Satisfaction has cascading effects on multiple organisational outcomes. The relationships are bidirectional and not always linear.

**Effect 1 — On Employee Performance (2 marks):**
- **Positive Correlation:** Satisfied employees are more engaged, energetic, and committed, leading to **higher productivity** (Harter, Schmidt, & Keyes, 2003 meta-analysis: correlation ≈ 0.30).
- **Counter-evidence:** Some studies (e.g., **Staw & Barsade, 1993**) suggest that satisfied employees may sometimes be **content with mediocrity**, showing lower drive.
- **Service Sector Example:** A satisfied **airline cabin crew member** is more likely to deliver warm, attentive service, leading to higher customer satisfaction scores (CSAT).

**Effect 2 — On Absenteeism (2 marks):**
- **Negative Correlation:** Higher job satisfaction is generally associated with **lower absenteeism**. Dissatisfied employees use sick leaves as a form of **withdrawal behaviour**.
- **Statistical Insight:** Each one-point drop in satisfaction (on a 5-point scale) has been associated with a **7-10% increase in unplanned leave** (based on European Service Sector studies).
- **Service Sector Example:** Dissatisfied **BPO employees** in call centres show high absenteeism during peak hours, disrupting customer service operations.

**Effect 3 — On Turnover (2 marks):**
- **Strong Negative Correlation:** Job satisfaction is one of the **best predictors of turnover intention** and actual quitting behaviour (Tett & Meyer, 1993).
- **Mathematical Relationship:** **Mobley's Intermediate Linkage Model** — Dissatisfaction → Thought of quitting → Search for alternatives → Evaluation → Intent → Actual turnover.
- **Service Sector Example:** India's **hospitality and retail sectors** suffer 30-40% annual attrition largely driven by low satisfaction with pay, shift timings, and supervisor behaviour.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Organisations must treat Job Satisfaction not as a "soft" HR metric but as a **strategic lever** for performance, attendance, and retention — especially critical in service industries where human capital is the primary differentiator.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Motivation with Satisfaction:** Students often write Vroom or Maslow as "satisfaction theories" without linking them to the satisfaction outcome. Always explicitly connect the theory to the **emotional/appraisal result**.
> 2. **One-Continuum Error:** In Herzberg's theory, students wrongly say "Satisfaction and Dissatisfaction are opposite ends of one scale." Mark deduction: **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the Equation:** For Vroom and Equity Theory, KTU awards full credit only when the **formula is shown and computed step-by-step**. Verbal explanations alone fetch partial credit.
> 4. **Ignoring Determinants:** Generic answers listing 2-3 determinants and stopping. Minimum **5 determinants** expected in a 7-mark question.
> 5. **No Real-World Examples:** In a 14-mark question, service sector/IT examples earn at least **1-1.5 extra marks** as per the latest KTU valuation key.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Job Satisfaction**

- **Definition (Locke, 1976):** Pleasurable emotional state from job appraisal — *memorise verbatim*.
- **Tri-component attitude model:** Cognitive + Affective + Behavioural.
- **5 Facet dimensions:** Pay, Work, Promotion, Supervision, Co-workers (Job Descriptive Index).
- **Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory:** Two separate continua, NOT one. Hygiene ≠ Motivator.
- **Maslow's 5-tier hierarchy:** Physiological → Safety → Social → Esteem → Self-Actualisation.
- **Vroom's Expectancy formula:** $M = E \times I \times V$ — must be computed numerically.
- **Adams' Equity formula:** Compare $O/I$ ratios; state the equity state explicitly.
- **Job Characteristics Model (JCM):** 5 dimensions → 3 psychological states → outcomes. Compute MPS using $\text{MPS} = (SV+TI+TS+A+F)/3$.
- **Measurement tools:** JDI, MSQ, Faces Scale, Spector's JSS, Single-Item Global Rating.
- **Effects:** Performance ↑, Absenteeism ↓, Turnover ↓ — supported by meta-analyses.
- **Distinguish clearly:** Job Satisfaction ≠ Job Involvement ≠ Organisational Commitment.
- **Indian context:** High attrition in IT/BPO/retail is satisfaction-driven; HR interventions must be **facet-specific**.
- **Modern relevance:** Equity Theory applies strongly to **hybrid work fairness**.
- **Critical Incident Method:** Used by Herzberg — interview-based qualitative technique.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: PERSONALITY — SECTION 1: CORE DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Personality** is defined as the *dynamic and organized set of characteristics possessed by a person that uniquely influences his or her cognitions, motivations, and behaviours in various situations* (adapted from the standard Organisational Behaviour frameworks endorsed by KTU 2024 scheme).

In the KTU 2024 scheme, Personality is treated as a **psychological construct** that determines how an individual reacts to the environment, peers, authority, and work pressure. It is the **sum total of ways in which an individual reacts to and interacts with others**. It is most often described in terms of measurable **traits** that a person exhibits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Academic Insight (KTU Examiner's Perspective):**
> Personality is **NOT** character, **NOT** temperament alone, and **NOT** physical appearance. It is a *psychological and behavioural construct* — a fusion of **internal (psychological)** and **external (social/environmental)** forces.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wardrobe & Outfit" Analogy

Imagine your personality is like a **personal wardrobe**:
- **Your wardrobe** = Personality (the complete collection of clothes you own).
- **The outfit you choose today** = Behaviour (the visible expression of your wardrobe in a particular context).
- **The occasion (interview, party, gym, funeral)** = Situation (which influences the *selection* of outfit from the wardrobe).

**The key insight:** Just as your wardrobe *limits* and *enables* what you can wear (you cannot wear a saree to a football match, even if you want to), your **personality *constrains* and *enables*** the *range* of behaviours available to you. The occasion (situation) decides *which outfit* (behaviour) you actually wear.

> **Formal Mapping for Engineering Students:**
> - Wardrobe → Personality structure
> - Outfit choice → Behaviour in a specific situation
> - Mirror feedback → Self-monitoring
> - New dress addition → Personality development over time

---

## 1.3 Why Personality Matters in Organisational Behaviour (OB)

| Context | Why Personality Matters |
|---|---|
| **Recruitment & Selection** | Personality tests predict job-fit, cultural-fit, and attrition risk. |
| **Team Building** | Diverse personality types balance task vs people orientation. |
| **Leadership** | Big Five traits predict leadership emergence and effectiveness. |
| **Conflict Management** | Personality explains why some individuals avoid conflict and others seek it. |
| **Stress & Burnout** | Neuroticism (Type A behaviour) is a strong predictor of occupational stress. |
| **Motivation** | Personality moderates the effect of motivators (e.g., Need for Achievement). |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight Box:**
> The KTU 2024 OBE framework explicitly maps Personality to **CO2 (Identify the behavioural dynamics in organisations)** and **CO4 (Apply personality and motivation theories to workplace scenarios).**

---

## 1.4 The "Person–Situation Debate" — A Foundational Premise

The single most important philosophical debate in Personality-OB literature is the **Person-Situation Debate**:

- **Dispositionist view (Allport, Cattell):** Behaviour is driven primarily by **internal traits**. A person who is honest at home is honest at work.
- **Situationist view (Mischel):** Behaviour is driven primarily by **external situations**. Even an honest person may lie under extreme duress.
- **Interactionist view (Modern KTU-recommended view):** Behaviour is the **result of the interaction** between personality *and* situation. **B = f(P, S)**, where B = Behaviour, P = Personality, S = Situation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite Line:**
> *"Personality is a result of both heredity and environment — neither operates in isolation."* This is a guaranteed 2–3 mark question in KTU exams.

---

## 1.5 The Four Core Characteristics of Personality (Definitions You MUST Memorise)

1. **Consistency** — A person generally behaves in a *similar* way across time. (e.g., An honest person is usually honest across situations.)
2. **Psychological and Behavioural** — Both *internal* (thoughts, feelings) and *external* (actions) are part of personality.
3. **Multiple Traits** — Personality is a *collection of many traits*; not a single dimension.
4. **Environmental Influence** — Family, culture, social setting, and life experiences *shape* personality.

---

## 1.6 Determinants of Personality — The Big Picture

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists **four major determinants**:

| Determinant | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Heredity (Nature)** | Genes, physical stature, facial features, energy levels | A child of a tall, athletic parent is more likely to be sporty. |
| **Environment (Nurture)** | Culture, family, social norms, life experiences | An Indian raised in Japan may show hybrid behavioural traits. |
| **Situation** | The specific context that triggers a behavioural response | The same person behaves differently in a wedding vs. a boardroom. |
| **Learning** | Conditioning, reinforcement, observation (Bandura's Social Learning) | A subordinate learns punctuality from a strict manager. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Determinants Formula (Memory Aid for KTU Exam):**
> **Personality = Heredity + Environment + Situation + Learning**
> Often remembered by the acronym **"HESL"** (Heritage-Environment-Situation-Learning).

---

# MODULE 2: PERSONALITY — SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FRAMEWORK SHEET

## 2.1 The Major Personality Theories (KTU 2024 Mandated Coverage)

The KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 2 mandates understanding of **four foundational theories** plus **two applied models**. Below is the comprehensive, exam-oriented breakdown.

---

### 2.2 Theory 1: Psychoanalytic Theory (Sigmund Freud)

Freud proposed that personality is composed of **three interacting systems**, often represented as an **iceberg model**.

| Component | Level | Function | Approximate Energy % |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Id** | Unconscious | Pleasure principle, primal urges, immediate gratification | ~60% |
| **Ego** | Conscious | Reality principle, mediates between Id and Superego | ~30% |
| **Superego** | Semi-conscious | Morality principle, ideals, conscience | ~10% |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** "**I**d = **I** want it now" (impulsive), "**E**go = **E**xecute sensibly" (rational), "**S**uperego = **S**hould I?" (moral).

**Freud's Stages of Psychosexual Development (Must-Memorise):**

1. **Oral (0–1 year)** — Issues: Weaning. Fixation leads to dependency or aggression.
2. **Anal (1–3 years)** — Issues: Toilet training. Fixation leads to anal-retentive (over-controlled) or anal-expulsive (messy) personality.
3. **Phallic (3–6 years)** — Issues: Oedipus/Electra complex. Fixation leads to authority issues.
4. **Latency (6–12 years)** — Issues: Socialisation, peer bonding.
5. **Genital (12+ years)** — Issues: Mature sexual and social relationships.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** *Freud's theory is largely un-falsifiable and is criticised for being non-scientific*. Modern OB courses (including KTU) treat it as a *historical foundation* rather than a predictive tool.

---

### 2.3 Theory 2: Trait Theory (Allport, Cattell, Eysenck)

**Core Premise:** Personality can be **measured** as a set of **stable, identifiable traits** that predict behaviour.

#### 2.3.1 Allport's Trait Taxonomy (1937)

- **Cardinal Traits** — Dominant, pervasive traits that define a person's life (e.g., Mother Teresa's selflessness). Rare.
- **Central Traits** — 5–10 major traits that form the basic building blocks (e.g., honesty, friendliness).
- **Secondary Traits** — Minor, situational preferences (e.g., preferring tea over coffee).

#### 2.3.2 Cattell's 16 Personality Factors (16PF)

Raymond Cattell used **factor analysis** to identify **16 source traits** that describe personality. Examples include: *Warmth (A), Reasoning (B), Emotional Stability (C), Dominance (E), Liveliness (F), Rule-Consciousness (G), Social Boldness (H), Sensitivity (I), Vigilance (L), Abstractedness (M), Privateness (N), Apprehension (O), Openness to Change (Q1), Self-Reliance (Q2), Perfectionism (Q3), Tension (Q4)*.

#### 2.3.3 Eysenck's PEN Model

Hans Eysenck proposed personality on **three super-traits**:
- **P**sychoticism (P) — Tough-mindedness, impulsivity, anti-social.
- **Extraversion (E)** — Sociable, lively, assertive.
- **Neuroticism (N)** — Anxious, depressed, moody, low self-esteem.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick for PEN:** "**P**eople **E**xpress **N**euroticism" — order matters: P → E → N.

---

### 2.4 Theory 3: Social Learning Theory (Bandura & Rotter)

#### 2.4.1 Albert Bandura — Reciprocal Determinism

Bandura rejected Freud's unconscious focus and proposed that personality is learned through **observation, imitation, and modelling**. His famous **Bobo Doll experiment** demonstrated that children imitate aggressive behaviour observed in adults.

**Reciprocal Determinism** states that personality is shaped by a *triadic interaction*:

$$B = f(P, E) \quad \text{(where B = Behaviour, P = Person factors, E = Environment)}$$

But also:

$$P = f(B, E), \quad E = f(P, B)$$

> All three are **mutually causal** — they influence each other simultaneously.

#### 2.4.2 Julian Rotter — Locus of Control

Rotter introduced a critical personality dimension:

- **Internal Locus of Control** — Believes outcomes are *within one's own control* ("I got promoted because I worked hard").
- **External Locus of Control** — Believes outcomes are *governed by fate, luck, or powerful others* ("I got promoted because the boss liked me").

| Locus Type | Workplace Implication |
|---|---|
| Internal | Higher job performance, more initiative, greater job satisfaction. |
| External | Lower initiative, higher learned helplessness, blames environment for failures. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Discuss Locus of Control with reference to organisational behaviour."* — Answer must include both internal/external distinction and a workplace example.

---

### 2.5 Theory 4: Humanistic Theory (Maslow & Rogers)

#### 2.5.1 Carl Rogers — Self-Concept Theory

Rogers argued that personality is shaped by the **gap between the Real Self and the Ideal Self**.

- **Real Self** — Who you *actually* are.
- **Ideal Self** — Who you *wish* to be.
- **Congruence** — When Real = Ideal → Psychological wellness.
- **Incongruence** — When Real ≠ Ideal → Anxiety, defensiveness, maladjustment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** In performance appraisals, employees experience *cognitive dissonance* when the feedback image (how others see them) clashes with their *self-image*.

#### 2.5.2 Abraham Maslow — Hierarchy of Needs (Preview)

While primarily a motivation theory, Maslow's needs hierarchy **informs personality development**: personality growth is the journey from physiological needs → safety → belonging → esteem → self-actualisation.

---

### 2.6 Applied Model 1: Myers-Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI)

Based on **Carl Jung's theory of psychological types**, the MBTI classifies people on **four dichotomies**:

| Dichotomy | Pole 1 | Pole 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Extraversion (E) | Introversion (I) |
| **Information Gathering** | Sensing (S) | Intuition (N) |
| **Decision Making** | Thinking (T) | Feeling (F) |
| **Lifestyle Orientation** | Judging (J) | Perceiving (P) |

This yields **16 personality types** (e.g., INTJ, ESFP). Each letter reflects a **preference**, not an ability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Workplace Application:** MBTI is widely used in team composition, leadership training, and career counselling. **Criticism:** It has poor **reliability** (people get different results on retest) and **validity** (does not predict job performance).

---

### 2.7 Applied Model 2: The Big Five Model (OCEAN) — THE MOST IMPORTANT MODEL FOR KTU EXAMS

The **Big Five** is the most empirically validated and academically respected personality framework. It is also known as the **Five-Factor Model (FFM)**.

| Trait | High-Scorer Behaviour | Low-Scorer Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **O**penness | Imaginative, curious, creative, open to new experiences | Conventional, practical, prefers routine |
| **C**onscientiousness | Disciplined, organised, reliable, achievement-oriented | Careless, disorganised, impulsive |
| **E**xtraversion | Sociable, talkative, assertive, energetic | Reserved, quiet, solitary |
| **A**greeableness | Trusting, cooperative, helpful, compassionate | Sceptical, competitive, antagonistic |
| **N**euroticism | Anxious, insecure, moody, emotionally unstable | Calm, secure, emotionally stable |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Acronym:** **OCEAN** or **CANOE** (Conscientiousness, Agreeableness, Neuroticism, Openness, Extraversion).

---

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Personality Theories Comparison

| Theory | Founder | Core Idea | Determinism? | Application in OB |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Psychoanalytic | Freud | Id, Ego, Superego | Biological/psychic | Understanding unconscious bias |
| Trait | Allport, Cattell, Eysenck | Stable traits predict behaviour | Genetic/biological | Personality testing, selection |
| Social Learning | Bandura, Rotter | Learning through observation | Environmental | Training, modelling behaviour |
| Humanistic | Rogers, Maslow | Self-actualisation & congruence | Free will | Counselling, EAPs |
| MBTI | Jung / Briggs-Myers | 4 dichotomies, 16 types | Free will preference | Team building, communication |
| Big Five (OCEAN) | Costa & McCrae | 5 super-traits | Trait-based | **Gold standard for selection, leadership, performance** |

---

### 2.9 Personality–Job Fit Theory (Holland's Hexagonal Model)

John Holland proposed that **occupational satisfaction** depends on the match between an individual's personality type and the work environment. He classified both people and jobs into **six types**:

1. **Realistic (R)** — Practical, physical, hands-on (e.g., engineer, mechanic).
2. **Investigative (I)** — Analytical, intellectual, research-oriented (e.g., scientist, R&D).
3. **Artistic (A)** — Expressive, creative, non-conformist (e.g., designer, writer).
4. **Social (S)** — Helping, teaching, empathetic (e.g., teacher, counsellor).
5. **Enterprising (E)** — Persuasive, ambitious, leadership-oriented (e.g., manager, lawyer).
6. **Conventional (C)** — Structured, detail-oriented, rule-following (e.g., accountant, clerk).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Holland's theory predicts that **congruence** between personality and occupation leads to higher satisfaction, tenure, and performance. **Hexagonal arrangement** shows that adjacent types are similar, opposite types are incompatible.

---

## 2.10 Cross-Theoretical Synthesis — The "Personality Iceberg" Modern View

Modern OB integrates the above theories into a layered view of personality:

| Layer | Visibility | Examples | Source Theory |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface Behaviour | Visible | Actions, speech, decisions | All theories |
| Conscious Values | Partially visible | Beliefs, attitudes, opinions | Humanistic, Social Learning |
| Self-Concept | Internal | Self-esteem, identity | Rogers |
| Core Traits | Deeply internal | Big Five, MBTI preferences | Trait Theory |
| Unconscious Drives | Hidden | Id impulses, childhood conflicts | Psychoanalytic |
| Biological Base | Hidden | Genetics, hormones, neurology | Heredity, Eysenck |
