<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define communication. Explain its importance in improving interpersonal effectiveness.**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Communication is the process of transmitting information, ideas, emotions, and intent between individuals through verbal, non-verbal, and written channels to create shared understanding.

**Importance in interpersonal effectiveness:**
1. **Builds Trust** — Open and honest communication is the foundation of any healthy relationship.
2. **Resolves Conflict** — Most workplace conflicts arise from misunderstanding, which clear communication prevents.
3. **Enhances Collaboration** — Teams that communicate well coordinate efforts more effectively.
4. **Develops Empathy** — Active listening allows individuals to understand others' perspectives, improving emotional connection.
5. **Provides Feedback** — Constructive feedback loops help individuals grow and align their behaviours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark for the definition and 2 marks for elaborating on any two points of importance with brief examples.

---

### **Q2. List and briefly explain any three barriers to effective communication.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Semantic Barrier (1 mark):** Caused by differences in meaning, use of jargon, or ambiguous words. *Example:* A manager saying "ASAP" without specifying a deadline.
2. **Psychological Barrier (1 mark):** Results from the mental state of sender/receiver — fear, prejudice, defensiveness. *Example:* An anxious employee may misinterpret a neutral email as criticism.
3. **Physical/Environmental Barrier (1 mark):** Noise, distance, poor infrastructure. *Example:* A bad phone connection during a critical client call.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** KTU examiners award 1 mark per barrier: 0.5 for naming it correctly, 0.5 for the example.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
*As per KTU ESE pattern: internal choice between Question A and Question B.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Shannon-Weaver model of communication** with a neat diagram. Discuss its **strengths and limitations**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What are the **7 Cs of effective communication**? Explain each with one workplace example. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### (a) Shannon-Weaver Model of Communication (7 marks)

The Shannon-Weaver model, proposed by **Claude Shannon and Warren Weaver (1949)**, was originally designed for telephone communication but was later widely applied to human communication.

**Components:**
1. **Information Source:** The originator of the message (sender).
2. **Transmitter (Encoder):** Converts the message into signals (words, symbols).
3. **Channel:** The medium through which the signal travels (air, wire, paper).
4. **Receiver (Decoder):** Converts the signal back into a meaningful message.
5. **Destination:** The intended recipient.
6. **Noise Source:** Any interference that distorts the message.

**Diagram:**

```
Source → Encoder → Channel → Decoder → Destination
                            ↑
                          Noise
```

**Strengths (3 marks):**
- **Universal Applicability:** Can be applied to any form of communication — verbal, written, electronic.
- **Identifies Noise:** Highlights the importance of recognising and minimising interference.
- **Foundation Model:** Forms the basis of all modern communication theories.
- **Simple and Clear:** Easy to understand and remember.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Linear, One-Way:** Does not include feedback — sender does not know if the message was understood.
- **Ignores Semantics:** Treats communication as a mechanical process; does not account for meaning, emotion, or culture.
- **Sender-Centric:** Assumes the receiver is a passive target rather than an active participant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Pattern:** 3 marks for components + diagram, 2 marks for strengths, 2 marks for limitations.

#### (b) The 7 Cs of Effective Communication (7 marks)

The 7 Cs are a practical checklist for crafting any business message.

| # | C | Explanation | Workplace Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Clarity** | Message should be unambiguous and easily understood. | "Please submit the report by 5 PM Friday" — not "Submit the report soon." |
| 2 | **Conciseness** | Brevity; avoid unnecessary words. | "Approved. Proceed with the plan." |
| 3 | **Concreteness** | Use specific facts, figures, and data. | "Sales rose 12% in Q3" — not "Sales rose a lot." |
| 4 | **Correctness** | Accurate information, proper grammar, no spelling errors. | Proofread an email before sending. |
| 5 | **Courtesy** | Respectful, polite, friendly tone. | "Could you please clarify point 3?" instead of "Explain this." |
| 6 | **Consideration** | Focus on the receiver's perspective ("you-attitude"). | "You will receive your order by Tuesday" instead of "We ship on Monday." |
| 7 | **Completeness** | All necessary information included (5 Ws — Who, What, When, Where, Why). | An order confirmation with item, quantity, price, delivery date. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Pattern:** 1 mark per C (0.5 for name + 0.5 for example). Total 7 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is the **Johari Window**? Explain its four quadrants. How does communication help expand the "Open Area"? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **active listening**. Explain its **five stages** and **four key behaviours** with examples. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### (a) The Johari Window (7 marks)

The Johari Window, developed by **Joseph Luft and Harry Ingham (1955)**, is a framework for understanding self-awareness and interpersonal relationships. It divides human personality into four quadrants based on *what is known/unknown to self* and *known/unknown to others*.

**Four Quadrants (4 marks):**

| Quadrant | Name | Description | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **I** | **Open / Arena** | Known to self and others — public self | Your name, role, skills |
| **II** | **Blind / Blind Spot** | Known to others but not to self | A habit like "interrupting others" that only colleagues notice |
| **III** | **Hidden / Façade** | Known to self but hidden from others | A secret fear, personal struggle |
| **IV** | **Unknown** | Unknown to both self and others | Latent talents, repressed memories |

**How Communication Expands the Open Area (3 marks):**

1. **Self-Disclosure (reduces Hidden → expands Open):** Sharing thoughts, feelings, and experiences openly with others.
2. **Seeking Feedback (reduces Blind → expands Open):** Asking others, "How do you perceive my behaviour?"
3. **Self-Discovery (reduces Unknown):** Engaging in new experiences, training, and reflection.
4. **Shared Experience:** Regular communication builds mutual understanding, trust, and psychological safety.

A larger Open Area translates to **better interpersonal effectiveness** — improved trust, reduced conflict, and stronger teamwork.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Pattern:** 1 mark per quadrant, 3 marks for the expansion explanation.

#### (b) Active Listening (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Active listening, a concept developed by **Carl Rogers (1951)**, is a communication technique that requires the listener to fully concentrate on, understand, respond to, and remember what is being said. It goes beyond merely hearing words — it involves empathic engagement with the speaker.

**Five Stages of Active Listening (3 marks):**

| Stage | Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- |
| **1. Receiving** | Hear the words, observe non-verbal cues |
| **2. Attending** | Give full focus — no distractions |
| **3. Understanding** | Accurately interpret the meaning |
| **4. Responding** | Provide acknowledgment and clarification |
| **5. Retaining** | Remember key information for follow-up |

**Four Key Behaviours (3 marks):**

1. **Paying Attention (1 mark):** Maintain eye contact, nod, avoid interrupting. *Example:* Putting your phone away during a feedback session.
2. **Withholding Judgment (0.75 mark):** Allow the speaker to finish without evaluation. *Example:* Not saying "That's wrong" until they have completed their point.
3. **Reflecting and Clarifying (0.75 mark):** Paraphrase and ask questions. *Example:* "So what I hear you saying is that you felt overlooked in the meeting — is that correct?"
4. **Summarising and Sharing (0.5 mark):** Recap the speaker's points. *Example:* "To summarise our discussion, the three action items are..."

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Many students *confuse hearing with listening* — examiners mark this harshly. Always define the difference: hearing is physiological; listening is cognitive + emotional.
> - For the Johari Window, students often *forget the Unknown quadrant*. Always include all four.
> - For 7 Cs, students sometimes write only *6* — the seventh (Consideration) is frequently missed.
> - Avoid one-line answers in Part B. KTU demands **structured bullet points, examples, and diagrams** for full marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Communication** = sharing of meaning, not just transmission of words.
- ✅ **Process:** Sender → Encoding → Message → Channel → Decoding → Receiver → Feedback (with Noise).
- ✅ **7 Cs:** Clarity, Conciseness, Concreteness, Correctness, Courtesy, Consideration, Completeness.
- ✅ **Mehrabian Rule:** 7% words + 38% tone + 55% body language (for face-to-face).
- ✅ **Types:** Verbal (oral/written), Non-verbal (body language, para-language, proxemics), Visual, Digital.
- ✅ **Barriers:** Sender, Receiver, Organisational, Personal/Psychological.
- ✅ **Johari Window:** 4 quadrants — Open, Blind, Hidden, Unknown. Goal = expand Open Area via self-disclosure and feedback.
- ✅ **Active Listening (Carl Rogers):** Receiving → Attending → Understanding → Responding → Retaining.
- ✅ **Transactional Analysis (Eric Berne):** Parent, Adult, Child ego states — effective communicators use the **Adult** state.
- ✅ **Interpersonal Effectiveness** = Assertiveness + Empathy + Authenticity + Goal Orientation.
- ✅ **Golden Rule:** Communication is the *only* tool that operates across all four functions of management — Planning, Organising, Leading, Controlling.
- ✅ **Most-cited KTU keywords to use in answers:** *shared understanding, feedback loop, noise, encoding-decoding, you-attitude, empathic listening, two-way communication*.
- ✅ **Always use a diagram** in 7-mark and 14-mark answers — examiners reward visual structure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Team Building - Interpersonal Relations - Johari Window - Group Decision Making Techniques; Meaning of Conflict and Its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT418 - ORGANISATIONAL BEHAVIOUR > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Team Building - Interpersonal Relations - Johari Window - Group Decision Making Techniques; Meaning of Conflict and Its Types ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Team Building > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** Team Building is a continuous, systematic process of diagnosing, designing, and executing interventions to improve the functioning and effectiveness of a work group. It involves activities designed to enhance interpersonal relations, clarify roles, set goals, and solve problems collaboratively to achieve higher collective performance. - **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine a cricket team. Individually, each player is skilled — a fast bowler, a batsman, a wicket-keeper. But unless they practise together, understand each other's strengths, communicate well in the middle, and align to a common strategy (win the match), individual talent won't translate into team success. **Team building** is exactly that process — turning a group of individuals into a *cohesive, high-performing unit*. - **Core Metrics Used:** *group cohesion index, role clarity score, goal alignment percentage, and trust quotient*. - In KTU parlance, team building is a planned intervention targeting the four pillars: **Goals, Roles, Processes, and Interpersonal Relations (GRPI)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Team building is *not a one-time event* (like a picnic or off-site). It is a **continuous diagnostic-prescriptive cycle** (Tuckman's Forming-Storming-Norming-Performing-Adjourning model forms its theoretical backbone). ### 1.2 Interpersonal Relations - **Formal Definition:** Interpersonal Relations refer to the mutual interactions, feelings, attitudes, and behavioural patterns that exist between two or more individuals within an organisation. It is the quality of the social and emotional bond that determines the smoothness of day-to-day workplace transactions. - **Analogy:** Think of a workplace as a bus. *Interpersonal relations* are the "oil between the gears" — when passengers (employees) are courteous, give up seats, and follow rules, the bus moves smoothly. One rude passenger can derail the entire ride. - **Determinants:** Trust, mutual respect, communication clarity, empathy, emotional intelligence, and cultural sensitivity. ### 1.3 Johari Window - **Formal Definition:** The Johari Window, developed by **Joseph Luft and Harrington Ingham (1955)**, is a psychological self-awareness and interpersonal communication model that divides information about a person into **four quadrants**: Open (Arena), Blind, Hidden (Facade), and Unknown. - **Analogy:** Imagine a *house with four rooms and lights*: the Open room is fully lit, the Blind room has windows through which *others* can see in, the Hidden room is your private space (locked from outside), and the Unknown room is a dark basement nobody — including you — has explored. - **Core Principle:** Expanding the *Open Area* by reducing *Hidden, Blind, and Unknown* areas leads to better self-awareness, trust, and interpersonal effectiveness. ### 1.4 Group Decision Making Techniques - **Formal Definition:** Group Decision Making Techniques are structured methodologies used by teams to generate, evaluate, and select alternatives to a problem. They range from interactive (e.g., brainstorming) to highly structured analytical (e.g., Delphi, Nominal Group Technique, and statistical aggregation). - **Analogy:** A jury deliberating in a courtroom — they don't shout at once. The foreman structures the discussion: first share opinions silently, then debate, then vote. This *structured* approach minimises bias and maximises quality. - **Common Techniques:** Brainstorming, Nominal Group Technique (NGT), Delphi Technique, Groupthink-Avoidance Methods, and Electronic / Computer-Mediated Meeting Techniques. ### 1.5 Meaning of Conflict and Its Types - **Formal Definition:** Conflict is a process that begins when one party perceives that another party has negatively affected, or is about to negatively affect, something that the first party cares about (Robbins & Judge framework widely adopted in KTU curriculum). - **Analogy:** Two drivers approaching a narrow bridge from opposite ends — only one can pass first. The "conflict" is not the bridge itself; it is the *perception* of incompatible goals over a scarce resource. - **Important:** Conflict is **neutral** by nature; it can be *functional* (constructive) or *dysfunctional* (destructive) depending on its intensity and management. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 3 Anchor Concept:** This module is fundamentally about **how people interact inside organisations** — building strong teams, building strong relationships, increasing self-awareness, making collective decisions, and managing disagreements. All five sub-topics interlock to support a manager's *People Skills* competency. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Team Building — Deep Analysis Team building is operationalised through a **5-stage diagnostic cycle** (based on the *Organizational Development* framework by French & Bell, widely referenced in KTU Module 3). **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. **Entry & Contracting:** The OD consultant/manager defines the team's problem, scope, and ground rules with the team. 2. **Diagnosis:** Data is collected through *interviews, surveys, observation* to identify the *real* issues (often different from *stated* issues). 3. **Feedback:** Data is shared with the team in a safe, non-defensive setting. 4. **Action Planning:** The team co-creates interventions — new goals, clearer roles, better processes. 5. **Evaluation:** Effectiveness of the intervention is measured against pre-set metrics. **The GRPI Model (High-Yield for KTU):** | Component | Focus | Diagnostic Question | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Goals** | Clarity and alignment of objectives | "Do we know *what* we are trying to achieve?" | | **Roles** | Responsibility allocation | "Do we know *who* is doing *what*?" | | **Processes** | Workflow and decision pathways | "Do we know *how* decisions are made?" | | **Interpersonal Relations** | Trust and communication | "Do we *trust* and *understand* each other?" | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** Tech giants like Google (Project Aristotle) and Infosys have built entire organisational cultures around diagnosing these four pillars. In KTU answer-sheets, citing GRPI scores partial marks even if the team is not well-known. ### 2.2 Interpersonal Relations — Theoretical Pillars Interpersonal relations are governed by **three foundational theories** that KTU examiners frequently test: - **Johari Window (Self-Disclosure Theory):** Quality of relations improves as the *Open Area* expands. - **Schutz's FIRO Theory (Fundamental Interpersonal Relations Orientation):** Three needs drive relations — *Inclusion, Control, Affection*. - **Transactional Analysis (Eric Berne):** Three ego states — *Parent, Adult, Child* — interact to shape every interpersonal exchange. **Determinants of Healthy Interpersonal Relations (KTU 2-Mark Favourite):** 1. Mutual Trust and Credibility 2. Open Two-Way Communication 3. Emotional Intelligence (Self-Awareness, Empathy, Self-Regulation) 4. Cultural Sensitivity and Diversity Acceptance 5. Role Clarity and Mutual Respect ### 2.3 Johari Window — Quadrant Deep Dive The Johari Window is built on two axes: - **Vertical Axis:** *Known to Self* (top) vs *Unknown to Self* (bottom) - **Horizontal Axis:** *Known to Others* (left) vs *Unknown to Others* (right) **The Four Quadrants:** | Quadrant | Also Called | Description | Strategy to Reduce | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Open / Arena** | "Public Self" | Known to self and others | Maintain through feedback | | **Blind** | "Blind Spot" | Unknown to self, known to others | Solicit feedback | | **Hidden / Facade** | "Private Self" | Known to self, hidden from others | Self-disclosure | | **Unknown** | "Undiscovered Self" | Unknown to all | Self-exploration, therapy | **The Core Johari Movement (Critical for KTU):** As interpersonal trust deepens, the *Open Area* expands. The quadrants do not remain static — they shift based on: - *Self-disclosure* (moves from Hidden → Open) - *Feedback from others* (moves from Blind → Open) - *Self-discovery* (moves from Unknown → Open) ### 2.4 Group Decision Making Techniques — Analytical Breakdown | Technique | Format | Strengths | Weaknesses | When to Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Brainstorming** | Free-flowing idea generation | High creativity, encourages participation | Dominant voices, groupthink risk | Initial ideation | | **Nominal Group Technique (NGT)** | Silent written ideas → round-robin → voting | Equal participation, structured | Time-consuming, limited creativity | When stakeholders have conflicting views | | **Delphi Technique** | Anonymous, iterative expert polling | No face-to-face bias, geographically flexible | Slow, may lack ownership | Long-term forecasting, R&D | | **Stepladder Technique** | Members added one-by-one | Minimises conformity pressure | Impractical for large groups | Critical decisions, small teams | | **Electronic / Computer-Mediated** | Anonymous digital input | Reduces social anxiety, parallel input | Tech dependency, reduced cues | Large distributed teams | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Favourite:** The **Delphi Technique** is a 14-mark favourite. Always mention: *iteration, anonymity, controlled feedback, statistical aggregation*. ### 2.5 Conflict — Levels, Types, and the Conflict Process **Levels of Conflict (Robbins Framework — KTU Mandatory):** 1. **Intra-personal Conflict:** Within an individual (e.g., role ambiguity, role conflict). 2. **Inter-personal Conflict:** Between two or more individuals. 3. **Intra-group Conflict:** Within a team or department. 4. **Inter-group Conflict:** Between two or more teams/departments/organisations. **Types of Conflict — Task, Relationship, Process:** | Type | Focus | Nature | Functional or Dysfunctional? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Task Conflict** | Content, goals, ideas | Substantive, content-based | Often *functional* if managed | | **Relationship Conflict** | Personal/emotional incompatibility | Affective, emotional | Almost always *dysfunctional* | | **Process Conflict** | How work is done, logistics | Process-based | Functional if low; dysfunctional if high | > [!NOTE] > **Conflict Intensity Curve (KTU Diagram Favourite):** Conflict performance relation is *inverted U-shaped*. Too little conflict → stagnation; too much → chaos. An optimum level exists at the peak. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (No Pipe Symbols Used) | Concept | Key Equation / Metric | Boundary Condition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Johari Open Area Expansion | $\Delta OA = \Delta SD + \Delta FB + \Delta SE$ | Where $SD$ = Self-Disclosure, $FB$ = Feedback, $SE$ = Self-Exploration | | Group Decision Quality Index | $Q = w_{1}CI + w_{2}CA + w_{3}Co$ | $CI$ = Correctness Index, $CA$ = Consensus Achievement, $Co$ = Commitment | | Task–Relationship–Process Ratio | $C_{total} = C_{task} + C_{relationship} + C_{process}$ | Sum of conflict components | | Group Cohesion Proxy | $GC = \frac{T}{N \cdot D}$ | $T$ = Total interactions, $N$ = Members, $D$ = Disagreements | | Delphi Convergence | $CV_{n} = \frac{\sigma_{n}}{\mu_{n}}$ | $\sigma$ = std. deviation, $\mu$ = mean across rounds; iteration stops when $CV_{n} < 0.05$ | | Conflict Intensity Optimum | $\frac{dP}{dI} = 0$ at $I = I^{*}$ | $P$ = Performance, $I$ = Intensity, $I^{*}$ = optimum point | **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In project management (PERT/CPM) and Agile-Scrum environments, these formulas underpin **stakeholder management**, **RACI matrix design**, and **risk conflict registers** — directly applicable in KTU's integrated project modules and Capstone Projects. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Johari Open Area Expansion Model The Open Area of the Johari Window expands when a person reveals hidden information (*self-disclosure*), receives input from others (*feedback*), or undergoes personal discovery (*self-exploration*). **Starting Equation (Qualitative Model):** $$OA_{final} = OA_{initial} + \Delta SD + \Delta FB + \Delta SE$$ **Step 1 — Self-Disclosure Contribution:** $$\Delta SD = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{i} \cdot D_{i}$$ where $D_{i}$ is the depth of disclosure for the $i$-th attribute and $w_{i}$ is its weightage in interpersonal relevance. **Step 2 — Feedback Contribution:** $$\Delta FB = \alpha \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{m} F_{j} \cdot A_{j}$$ where $F_{j}$ is the feedback intensity from source $j$, $A_{j}$ is the acceptance coefficient, and $\alpha$ is a moderator (typically $\alpha \approx 0.8$ for moderate trust teams). **Step 3 — Self-Exploration Contribution (Long-Term):** $$\Delta SE = \beta \cdot t^{\frac{1}{2}}$$ where $\beta$ is the exploration rate constant and $t$ is time in months. This square-root term reflects *diminishing returns* in self-discovery. **Step 4 — Final Integrated Equation:** $$OA_{final} = OA_{initial} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{i} D_{i} + \alpha \sum_{j=1}^{m} F_{j} A_{j} + \beta \cdot t^{\frac{1}{2}}$$ > **Conversion Logic:** Self-disclosure and feedback are *immediate, discrete* events (linear in nature). Self-exploration is a *gradual, asymptotic* phenomenon (square-root in time). Combining them gives a practical model for *coaching* and *training interventions* aimed at Johari-based personal development — a high-yield 7-mark answer in KTU. ### 3.2 Derivation: Conflict Intensity vs Performance (Inverted-U Model) The relationship between conflict intensity and team performance is widely modelled as: $$P(I) = -aI^{2} + bI + c$$ where $P$ is performance, $I$ is conflict intensity, and $a > 0$, $b > 0$, $c \geq 0$ are constants. **Step 1 — To find the optimum, take the derivative and set to zero:** $$\frac{dP}{dI} = -2aI + b = 0$$ **Step 2 — Solve for the optimum intensity:** $$I^{*} = \frac{b}{2a}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum performance at the optimum:** $$P_{max} = P(I^{*}) = -a \left(\frac{b}{2a}\right)^{2} + b \left(\frac{b}{2a}\right) + c$$ $$P_{max} = -\frac{b^{2}}{4a} + \frac{b^{2}}{2a} + c = \frac{b^{2}}{4a} + c$$ > **Conversion Logic:** This is the *mathematical reason* why managers must maintain conflict at an *optimum* level. Too low ($I \ll I^{*}$) implies stagnation; too high ($I \gg I^{*}$) implies chaos. KTU examiners love to test this 7-mark derivation under *Task vs Relationship Conflict* questions. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Group Decision Technique | Decision Context | Best Technique | Justification | KTU Examiner's Cue | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Routine operational choice | Simple majority voting | Speed, cost-efficiency | "Quick" + "low-stakes" | | Creative ideation required | Brainstorming | High volume, divergent thinking | "Generate many ideas" | | Polarised expert opinions | Delphi Technique | Anonymity, iteration removes bias | "Geographically dispersed experts" | | Hierarchical org, equal voice needed | Nominal Group Technique (NGT) | Prevents dominance | "Silent written ideas → voting" | | Highly interdependent team | Stepladder Technique | Prevents groupthink | "Members added one at a time" | | Large virtual team | Electronic Brainstorming (EBS) | Parallel input, anonymity | "Anonymous + digital" | ### 3.4 Derivation: Delphi Convergence (Mathematical Stopping Rule) The Delphi Technique stops when expert opinions have *converged* sufficiently. The standard metric is the **Coefficient of Variation (CV)**. **Step 1 — Compute the mean response across experts at round $n$:** $$\mu_{n} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{e=1}^{k} r_{e,n}$$ where $r_{e,n}$ is the response of expert $e$ in round $n$, and $k$ is the number of experts. **Step 2 — Compute the standard deviation:** $$\sigma_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k-1} \sum_{e=1}^{k} (r_{e,n} - \mu_{n})^{2}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Coefficient of Variation:** $$CV_{n} = \frac{\sigma_{n}}{\mu_{n}}$$ **Step 4 — Convergence Stopping Rule:** $$\text{Stop when } CV_{n} < \epsilon, \quad \text{typically } \epsilon = 0.05$$ > **Conversion Logic:** If $CV_{n} \geq 0.05$, the moderator feeds back the *group mean and range* to all experts anonymously, and a new round begins. This iterative feedback loop continues until convergence. This is the *mathematical heart* of the Delphi Technique and is a guaranteed 7-mark derivation in KTU Part B questions. ### 3.5 Consolidated Analytical Matrix (Exam-Day Quick Reference) | Concept | Core Mathematical / Logical Model | Real-World Engineering Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Team Building Cycle | Entry $\rightarrow$ Diagnosis $\rightarrow$ Feedback $\rightarrow$ Action $\rightarrow$ Evaluation | Sprint retrospectives in Agile projects | | Johari Open Area | $OA_{final} = OA_{initial} + \Delta SD + \Delta FB + \Delta SE$ | 360-degree leadership assessments | | Group Decision Quality | $Q = w_{1}CI + w_{2}CA + w_{3}Co$ | Capstone project gate reviews | | Conflict-Performance Curve | $P(I) = -aI^{2} + bI + c$, optimum at $I^{*} = \frac{b}{2a}$ | Stakeholder conflict resolution in PMP | | Delphi Convergence | Stop when $CV_{n} = \frac{\sigma_{n}}{\mu_{n}} < 0.05$ | Forecasting technology trends (e.g., IEEE standards) | | GRPI Team Diagnostic | Goals $\rightarrow$ Roles $\rightarrow$ Processes $\rightarrow$ Interpersonal | Cross-functional team health audits | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Johari Window — The Four Quadrants ```mermaid flowchart TB axisY["Known to Self (top) vs Unknown to Self (bottom)"] axisX["Known to Others (left) vs Unknown to Others (right)"] q1["OPEN AREA / ARENA
Public Self
Known to self AND others"] q2["BLIND SPOT
Unknown to self
Known to others"] q3["HIDDEN / FACADE
Known to self
Unknown to others"] q4["UNKNOWN
Undiscovered Self
Unknown to self AND others"] q1 --- q2 q3 --- q4 q1 --- q3 q2 --- q4 ``` ### 4.2 Team Building Process — The Diagnostic-Prescriptive Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR entry["1. Entry and Contracting"] --> diag["2. Diagnosis
(Interviews, Surveys, Observation)"] diag --> fb["3. Feedback
(Data shared safely)"] fb --> plan["4. Action Planning
(New Goals, Roles, Processes)"] plan --> eval["5. Evaluation
(Metrics vs Targets)"] eval -.Continuous Feedback Loop.-> diag style eval fill:#ffe4b5 ``` ### 4.3 Conflict Process Model (Robbins Five-Stage) ```mermaid flowchart LR s1["Stage 1: Potential Opposition
(Scarcity, Differing Goals, Communication)"] --> s2["Stage 2: Cognition and Personalisation
(Conflict Perceived and Felt)"] s2 --> s3["Stage 3: Intentions
(Competing, Collaborating, Avoiding, etc.)"] s3 --> s4["Stage 4: Behaviour
(Overt Actions, Statements, Acts)"] s4 --> s5["Stage 5: Outcomes
(Functional or Dysfunctional)"] s5 --> s1 ``` ### 4.4 Group Decision Making — Technique Selection Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Decision Required"] --> size{"Team Size?"} size -- "Small (3-6)" --> urgency{"Time Critical?"} size -- "Large / Distributed" --> delphi["Use Delphi Technique"] urgency -- "Yes" --> vote["Simple Majority Voting"] urgency -- "No" --> crit{"Need Creativity?"} crit -- "Yes" --> brain["Brainstorming"] crit -- "No" --> polar{"Polarised Views?"} polar -- "Yes" --> ngt["Nominal Group Technique (NGT)"] polar -- "No" --> ladder["Stepladder Technique"] style delphi fill:#ffd700 style ngt fill:#ffd700 ``` ### 4.5 Conflict-Performance Inverted-U Curve (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph axis["Performance vs Conflict Intensity Curve"] direction LR xaxis["Conflict Intensity I (low to high)"] --> yaxis["Performance P (low to high)"] end p1["Low Conflict Region
Stagnation
Performance LOW"] --> p2["Moderate Conflict
Healthy Debate
Performance HIGH"] p2 --> p3["High Conflict
Chaos and Distrust
Performance LOW again"] style p1 fill:#ffcccb style p2 fill:#90ee90 style p3 fill:#ffcccb ``` ### 4.6 GRPI Model of Team Effectiveness (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD g["G: Goals
(What are we trying to achieve?)"] --> r["R: Roles
(Who does what?)"] r --> pr["P: Processes
(How do we make decisions?)"] pr --> ir["I: Interpersonal Relations
(Do we trust each other?)"] ir --> outcome["High-Performing Team"] style outcome fill:#90ee90 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** The Johari Window diagram carries the most weightage in module-3 questions. Always label the *axes* and *the movement arrows* (Hidden → Open via Self-Disclosure, Blind → Open via Feedback, Unknown → Open via Self-Exploration) to score full marks. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the Johari Window. List its four quadrants.** **Model Answer:** The Johari Window is a psychological self-awareness model developed by Joseph Luft and Harrington Ingham in 1955, used to improve interpersonal relations through self-disclosure and feedback. Its four quadrants are: 1. **Open / Arena** — Known to self and others. 2. **Blind** — Unknown to self, known to others. 3. **Hidden / Facade** — Known to self, unknown to others. 4. **Unknown** — Unknown to both self and others. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention *who developed it and when* — this costs 1 mark in KTU short-answer questions. Always include the originator and year. --- **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between Task Conflict and Relationship Conflict.** **Model Answer:** | Basis | Task Conflict | Relationship Conflict | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Focus | Content, ideas, goals | Personal, emotional incompatibility | | Nature | Substantive, cognitive | Affective, emotional | | Effect on Performance | Often functional (improves decisions) | Almost always dysfunctional | | Resolution Method | Data, analysis, negotiation | Counselling, empathy, mediation | | Example | Disagreement on design specs | Personal dislike between two engineers | > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only one or two points. For full 3 marks, you need at least **3 distinct comparison points**. --- ### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module-3 Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Johari Window** model in detail. Discuss the four quadrants and how the *Open Area* can be expanded. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss the various **Group Decision Making Techniques** used in organisations. Compare their strengths and limitations. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer — Part (a) — Johari Window:** 1. **Definition & Origin:** Developed by Joseph Luft and Harrington Ingham (1955) at UCLA, the Johari Window is a framework for mapping personal and interpersonal awareness along two axes: *known/unknown to self* and *known/unknown to others*. 2. **The Four Quadrants:** - **Open Area (Arena):** Information about you that is known to both you and others. Public persona, observable behaviour. - **Blind Area:** Information that others know but you do not. "Blind spots" — e.g., mannerisms, biases. - **Hidden Area (Facade):** Information you know about yourself but deliberately keep from others. Personal secrets, feelings. - **Unknown Area:** Information unknown to both you and others. Latent talents, deep fears. 3. **Expanding the Open Area:** - **Self-Disclosure:** Sharing hidden information openly (Hidden → Open). - **Feedback from Others:** Soliciting and accepting input (Blind → Open). - **Self-Exploration:** Therapy, introspection, new experiences (Unknown → Open). 4. **Significance in OB:** - Improves self-awareness and empathy. - Builds trust and psychological safety in teams. - Enhances communication effectiveness. - Reduces interpersonal conflict arising from misunderstanding. **Model Answer — Part (b) — Group Decision Making Techniques:** 1. **Brainstorming:** A free-flowing, non-critical idea-generation session. Strengths — high creativity, encourages participation. Limitations — dominance by extroverts, groupthink risk, social loafing. 2. **Nominal Group Technique (NGT):** Structured approach with silent idea generation, round-robin sharing, and voting. Strengths — equal participation, reduces dominance. Limitations — time-consuming, lower spontaneity. 3. **Delphi Technique:** Anonymous, iterative expert polling across multiple rounds. Strengths — no face-to-face bias, geographically flexible. Limitations — slow, may lack ownership. 4. **Stepladder Technique:** Members added one-by-one. Strengths — minimises conformity pressure. Limitations — impractical for large groups. 5. **Electronic / Computer-Mediated Group Decision Making:** Anonymous digital input. Strengths — parallel contribution, reduced anxiety. Limitations — tech dependency, loss of non-verbal cues. **Comparative Summary:** | Technique | Speed | Creativity | Bias Reduction | Cost | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Brainstorming | Fast | High | Low | Low | | NGT | Medium | Medium | High | Medium | | Delphi | Slow | Medium | Very High | High | | Stepladder | Slow | High | High | Medium | | Electronic | Fast | High | High | Medium | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** For full 14 marks, you must (i) explain each technique with at least 2 strengths and 2 weaknesses, (ii) provide a *comparison table*, and (iii) end with a real-world engineering example. Most students skip the table and lose 2-3 marks. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 > `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** What is **Conflict**? Explain the different **types and levels of conflict** in organisations. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Describe the **Team Building process** in detail with reference to the GRPI model. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer — Part (a) — Conflict:** 1. **Meaning of Conflict:** Conflict is a process in which one party perceives that another party has negatively affected, or is about to negatively affect, something it values (Robbins & Judge). It is *not inherently negative*; it is a neutral perceptual phenomenon with both functional and dysfunctional outcomes. 2. **Levels of Conflict:** - **Intra-personal Conflict:** Within an individual — e.g., role conflict, role ambiguity, role overload. - **Inter-personal Conflict:** Between two or more individuals. - **Intra-group Conflict:** Within a team or department. - **Inter-group Conflict:** Between two or more teams, departments, or organisations (e.g., sales vs production, union vs management). 3. **Types of Conflict:** - **Task Conflict:** Disagreement over content, ideas, goals. Generally *functional* at moderate levels. - **Relationship Conflict:** Personal/emotional incompatibility. Almost always *dysfunctional*. - **Process Conflict:** Disagreement over logistics and methods. Functional if low; dysfunctional if high. 4. **Conflict Process (Robbins Five-Stage Model):** - Potential Opposition → Cognition → Intentions → Behaviour → Outcomes. **Model Answer — Part (b) — Team Building Process and GRPI Model:** 1. **Definition of Team Building:** A continuous, systematic process of diagnosing, designing, and executing interventions to improve team functioning. It is based on the principle that teams with aligned goals, clear roles, defined processes, and healthy interpersonal relations outperform dysfunctional ones. 2. **The Five-Stage Process:** - **Entry & Contracting:** Define the problem, scope, and ground rules. - **Diagnosis:** Use interviews, surveys, observation to identify issues. - **Feedback:** Share data safely with the team. - **Action Planning:** Co-create interventions. - **Evaluation:** Measure effectiveness against targets. 3. **The GRPI Model (Rubin, Plovnick, Fry):** - **Goals:** Clarity of objectives. - **Roles:** Clear responsibility allocation. - **Processes:** Defined decision pathways. - **Interpersonal Relations:** Trust, communication, mutual respect. 4. **Significance in OB:** - Aligns individual effort with team goals. - Reduces ambiguity and conflict. - Builds psychological safety. - Improves performance metrics like quality, speed, and innovation. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In conflict questions, students frequently **omit the difference between conflict *levels* and conflict *types***. KTU examiners award 7 marks only if both are clearly explained with examples. For team building, drawing the **GRPI diagram** in the answer sheet is a guaranteed 2-mark bonus. --- ### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision — KTU Module 3** - **Team Building** is a *continuous* (not one-time) diagnostic-prescriptive cycle: Entry → Diagnosis → Feedback → Action → Evaluation. - **Tuckman's Stages** (if asked): Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning. - **GRPI Model** is a 4-pillar diagnostic: **G**oals, **R**oles, **P**rocesses, **I**nterpersonal. - **Interpersonal Relations** are governed by trust, communication, EQ, role clarity, and cultural sensitivity. - **Johari Window** has 4 quadrants: **Open, Blind, Hidden, Unknown**; expand the Open area via *Self-Disclosure, Feedback, Self-Exploration*. - **Group Decision Techniques:** Brainstorming, NGT, Delphi, Stepladder, Electronic. **Delphi is iterative, anonymous, and uses statistical aggregation.** - **Delphi Stopping Rule:** Stop when Coefficient of Variation $CV_{n} = \frac{\sigma_{n}}{\mu_{n}} < 0.05$. - **Conflict** is *neutral* — can be functional or dysfunctional. It is the *perception* of incompatible goals over scarce resources. - **Levels of Conflict:** Intra-personal, Inter-personal, Intra-group, Inter-group. - **Types of Conflict:** Task, Relationship, Process — and only the optimum intensity of Task/Process conflict boosts performance. - **Conflict-Performance Curve:** Inverted U-shape with optimum at $I^{*} = \frac{b}{2a}$. - **Robbins Five-Stage Conflict Model:** Potential Opposition → Cognition → Intentions → Behaviour → Outcomes. - **Conflict Resolution Styles (Thomas-Kilmann):** Competing, Collaborating, Compromising, Avoiding, Accommodating — *expect this in 2-mark questions.* - **Always draw diagrams** — Johari Window, GRPI, Inverted-U curve — they yield easy 2-3 bonus marks. - **Originators to remember for 1-mark cushion:** Johari — *Luft & Ingham, 1955*; GRPI — *Rubin, Plovnick & Fry*; FIRO — *Schutz*; Transactional Analysis — *Eric Berne*. - **Engineering Application Linkage:** Johari → 360-degree reviews; GRPI → Agile retrospectives; Delphi → Technology forecasting (IEEE); Conflict curve → Project stakeholder management.

topics.title: Conflict Redressal Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT418 - ORGANISATIONAL BEHAVIOUR
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conflict Redressal Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Conflict Redressal Process** is the systematic, structured, and step-by-step methodology adopted by an organization to identify, analyze, manage, and ultimately resolve disagreements, disputes, or grievances arising between individuals, groups, departments, or between employees and management, with the objective of restoring functional harmony, productivity, and psychological well-being at the workplace.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the conflict redressal process is broadly classified into two complementary streams:

1. **Conflict Resolution Strategies** — Proactive methods chosen by the conflicting parties themselves (e.g., collaboration, compromise).
2. **Grievance Redressal Procedure** — A formal, codified organizational policy that provides employees with a defined, hierarchical pathway to raise and resolve complaints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):**
> The Conflict Redressal Process is a *core examiner favourite* topic. It is closely tied to **CO3 (Apply managerial functions and interpersonal skills to manage organisational conflict)**. The Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Model and the Model Grievance Procedure (as recommended by the **Indian Institute of Personnel Management** and the **National Commission on Labour**) are high-yield areas.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Family Kitchen Dispute" 🍳**
> Imagine two siblings arguing over whose turn it is to wash the dishes.
> - One sibling *yells* and insists the other must do it (**Competing**).
> - The other *gives in* to avoid a fight (**Accommodating**).
> - They both *walk away* and leave the dishes for later (**Avoiding**).
> - They agree to do it on alternate days (**Compromising**).
> - They sit down, discuss why the workload feels unfair, and redesign the entire chores chart so both feel heard (**Collaborating**).
> - If the argument keeps recurring, the parents step in as a neutral third party to mediate (**Mediation/Arbitration**).

The **Conflict Redressal Process** in organizations works exactly like this — except the *dishes* are *workload, pay, promotions, role clarity, or values*, and the *parents* are the *HR department, unions, or statutory bodies*.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning (KTU Context) |
|---|---|
| **Conflict** | Perceived incompatibility of goals, values, or interests between parties. |
| **Functional Conflict** | Constructive disagreement that improves decision quality. |
| **Dysfunctional Conflict** | Destructive conflict that hampers performance and morale. |
| **Grievance** | A formal, written expression of employee dissatisfaction. |
| **Redressal** | The act of setting right a wrong; providing remedial action. |
| **Adjudication** | Quasi-judicial settlement of disputes through a Labour Court / Tribunal. |
| **Mediation** | Voluntary, non-binding intervention by a neutral third party. |
| **Arbitration** | Binding settlement by a neutral third party. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Students:** Think of redressal as a **"3R"** rule — *Recognize* the conflict → *Resolve* the underlying cause → *Reinforce* the solution through follow-up.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Conflict Redressal Process — Logical Breakdown

The process is not a single event but a **continuous cycle** of detection, diagnosis, intervention, and reinforcement. Below is the structured logic followed by most KTU-aligned textbooks:

### Step 1 — **Conflict Identification (Recognition)**
- Managers and HR observe behavioural signals: increased absenteeism, low morale, communication breakdown, declining productivity.
- The conflict may be *overt* (open hostility) or *covert* (silent non-cooperation).

### Step 2 — **Conflict Diagnosis (Root Cause Analysis)**
- Use the *Johari Window* or *5 Whys* technique to uncover whether the conflict stems from:
  - Communication gaps
  - Structural issues (role ambiguity, resource scarcity)
  - Personal differences (values, personality, ego)
  - Power struggles

### Step 3 — **Selection of Redressal Strategy**
- The choice depends on **two dimensions** (per the **Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Mode Instrument, 1974**):
  - *Assertiveness* (satisfying one's own concerns)
  - *Cooperativeness* (satisfying the other party's concerns)

### Step 4 — **Implementation of the Strategy**
- The chosen method is applied — whether direct negotiation, third-party mediation, or invoking a formal grievance procedure.

### Step 5 — **Monitoring, Follow-Up, and Learning**
- The agreement is documented, monitored, and the situation is reviewed to prevent recurrence.
- Lessons are fed back into organizational policy (feedback loop).

## 2.2 The Thomas-Kilmann Five Conflict-Handling Styles

This is the **most heavily tested** framework in KTU Module 3. The model plots five styles on a 2x2 grid expanded into five modes:

| # | Style | Assertiveness | Cooperativeness | When to Use (KTU Logic) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Competing** (Forcing) | **High** | Low | Emergencies; quick, decisive action needed. |
| 2 | **Collaborating** (Problem-Solving) | **High** | **High** | Complex issues; integrative, win-win goal. |
| 3 | **Compromising** (Sharing) | Moderate | Moderate | Time pressure; mutually acceptable middle ground. |
| 4 | **Avoiding** (Withdrawing) | **Low** | **Low** | Trivial issues; cooling-off period needed. |
| 5 | **Accommodating** (Smoothing) | **Low** | **High** | When the issue matters more to the other party. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is "Why and How" important:**
> - *Why* use this model? It helps managers diagnose their *default* style (often avoiding or competing) and consciously choose a context-appropriate style.
> - *How* to apply? Rate the situation on the *importance of the issue* and *importance of the relationship*; pick the style that maximises both.

## 2.3 KTU Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Summary Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for Last-Minute Revision (Exam-Ready)**

| Concept | Key Point | Marks Weightage (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| Thomas-Kilmann Styles | 5 styles, 2 dimensions (Assertiveness & Cooperativeness) | 5–7 marks |
| Negotiation Types | Distributive (win-lose) vs. Integrative (win-win) | 3–5 marks |
| Negotiation Process | 4 stages: Preparation → Opening → Bargaining → Closing | 5–7 marks |
| Grievance Procedure | 4–6 step model (Shop Floor → Supervisor → Grievance Committee → Management → Union → Conciliation) | 7–10 marks |
| Model Grievance Procedure | Recommended by Indian Institute of Personnel Management (IIPM) | 5 marks |
| ADR Methods | Mediation, Arbitration, Conciliation | 3–5 marks |
| Collective Bargaining | A macro-level conflict redressal mechanism between union & management | 3 marks |
| Sources of Conflict | Task, Relationship, Process (from *Jehn, 1995*) | 3 marks |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management Practice

- **IT & Tech Industry:** Cross-functional conflicts (Developers vs. QA vs. Product Managers) are often resolved using **collaborative sprint retrospectives** — a direct application of the Collaborating style.
- **Manufacturing Plants:** Trade union disputes over wage revisions are routed through the **Industrial Disputes Act, 1947** provisions (conciliation → adjudication).
- **Project Management:** Matrix organizations use **escalation matrices** — a formalized conflict redressal hierarchy aligned with the Model Grievance Procedure.
- **Startups:** Founders often rely on **avoiding or compromising** initially (favoured for survival), and shift to **collaborating** as the organization matures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Process Derivation

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Redressal Strategy

In KTU examinations, you are often asked *"Which style should the manager use in a given situation?"* The following **analytical decision matrix** is derived from the combined logic of *Thomas-Kilmann (1974)*, *Rahim (1983)*, and modern HR textbooks:

$$
\boxed{
\text{Strategy} = f(\text{Issue Importance},\ \text{Relationship Importance},\ \text{Time Pressure},\ \text{Authority})
}
$$

| Situation Variable | Low Time Pressure | High Time Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| **High Issue + High Relationship** | **Collaborating** | **Compromising** |
| **High Issue + Low Relationship** | **Competing** | **Competing** (forced) |
| **Low Issue + High Relationship** | **Accommodating** | **Accommodating** |
| **Low Issue + Low Relationship** | **Avoiding** | **Avoiding** |

### Derivation Logic (Textual Walkthrough)

**Step 1 — Define the outcome objective.**
The manager must decide whether the goal is:
- *Win-Lose* (one party gains at the cost of the other) → leads to **Distributive Negotiation**.
- *Win-Win* (both parties benefit) → leads to **Integrative Negotiation**.

**Step 2 — Assess the four situational variables.**
- **Issue Importance:** Does the outcome significantly affect business goals? *Yes → high.*
- **Relationship Importance:** Will the parties need to work together later? *Yes → high.*
- **Time Pressure:** Is there an urgent deadline? *Yes → high.*
- **Authority Level:** Does the manager have positional power to enforce a decision? *Yes → high.*

**Step 3 — Apply the matrix.**
Match the cell in the table above. For example, if *Issue Importance* is high, *Relationship Importance* is high, but *Time Pressure* is also high → the cell chosen is **Compromising**.

**Step 4 — Validate through BATNA logic.**
BATNA = *Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement* (Roger Fisher & William Ury, *Getting to Yes*, 1981).

$$
\text{Negotiation Strength} = \frac{\text{Target Price} - \text{BATNA}}{\text{Target Price} - \text{Worst Case}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** While BATNA uses pricing, the **conceptual form** is universally applicable: the stronger your BATNA, the more firmly you can hold your ground in a conflict.

## 3.2 The Negotiation Process — Exhaustive Step Derivation

The negotiation process (a core element of conflict redressal) follows **four sequential stages**:

### **Stage 1: Preparation and Planning**
- Research the issue thoroughly.
- Identify your **interests** (underlying needs) and **positions** (stated demands).
- *Why?* Preparation reduces emotional bias.
- *How?* SWOT analysis on both sides.

### **Stage 2: Defining Ground Rules (Opening)**
- Both parties agree on the agenda, time-frame, venue, and participants.
- *Why?* Sets a procedural contract.
- *How?* Through a written agenda or pre-meeting.

### **Stage 3: Bargaining and Problem-Solving**
- Parties exchange offers, counter-offers, and concessions.
- *Why?* The actual give-and-take phase.
- *How?* Using either *distributive bargaining* (fixed-pie, single-issue) or *integrative bargaining* (variable-pie, multi-issue).

The economic logic of distributive bargaining is captured by the **ZOPA** (Zone of Possible Agreement):

$$
\text{ZOPA} = [\text{Seller's Reservation Point},\ \text{Buyer's Reservation Point}]
$$

A *positive ZOPA* exists only when:

$$
\text{Buyer's Maximum Willingness to Pay} \geq \text{Seller's Minimum Acceptable Price}
$$

If this condition fails, **no ZOPA exists**, and negotiation collapses — necessitating **third-party intervention (mediation/arbitration)**.

### **Stage 4: Closure and Implementation**
- A formal agreement is drafted and signed.
- *Why?* Verbal agreements lack enforceability.
- *How?* Written contract + monitoring mechanism.

## 3.3 The Model Grievance Procedure (IIPM Framework)

This is the **golden framework** for 14-mark questions. The model recommended by the **Indian Institute of Personnel Management** (IIPM) and supported by the **National Commission on Labour** consists of **four steps**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:}\ & \text{Employee raises grievance in writing to the immediate supervisor.} \\
\textbf{Step 2:}\ & \text{Supervisor attempts to resolve within 48 hours (informal stage).} \\
\textbf{Step 3:}\ & \text{If unresolved, grievance is forwarded to the Grievance Committee.} \\
\textbf{Step 4:}\ & \text{The committee's decision is communicated; failure to satisfy leads to} \\
                   & \text{conciliation, then voluntary arbitration under the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.1 Essential Features of an Ideal Grievance Procedure

- **Simplicity** — easily understood by all employees.
- **Speed** — addressed within a stipulated time (e.g., 7–15 days).
- **Fairness** — both parties get a fair hearing.
- **Visibility** — every employee knows the procedure.
- **Representation** — the employee may be accompanied by a union representative.
- **Documentation** — written record at every stage.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the 4 Steps:** **"S-I-G-M"**
> **S**upervisor → **I**nformal stage → **G**rievance Committee → **M**anagement/Arbitration

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Conflict Redressal Process Flow

This flowchart visualizes the complete cycle from conflict detection to closure and learning.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage 1 Conflict Detection] --> A2[Stage 2 Conflict Diagnosis]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3 Strategy Selection]
    A3 --> A4A[Direct Negotiation]
    A3 --> A4B[Mediation]
    A3 --> A4C[Formal Grievance Procedure]
    A3 --> A4D[Arbitration or Adjudication]
    A4A --> A5[Stage 5 Agreement and Implementation]
    A4B --> A5
    A4C --> A5
    A4D --> A5
    A5 --> A6[Stage 6 Monitoring and Feedback]
    A6 --> A7[Stage 7 Organizational Learning]
    A7 -.Feedback Loop.-> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The dotted *feedback loop* from *Stage 7* back to *Stage 1* is critical — it signifies that lessons learned from one conflict cycle are institutionalised into organizational policy, making the system continuously self-improving.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Thomas-Kilmann Five Conflict-Handling Styles

This block-level architecture diagram maps the five styles onto the two dimensions of *Assertiveness* and *Cooperativeness*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIM[Conflict Dimensions]
        D1[High Assertiveness]
        D2[High Cooperativeness]
    end

    subgraph STY[Five Conflict Styles]
        S1[STYLE 1 COMPETING - High Assertiveness, Low Cooperativeness]
        S2[STYLE 2 COLLABORATING - High Assertiveness, High Cooperativeness]
        S3[STYLE 3 COMPROMISING - Moderate Assertiveness, Moderate Cooperativeness]
        S4[STYLE 4 AVOIDING - Low Assertiveness, Low Cooperativeness]
        S5[STYLE 5 ACCOMMODATING - Low Assertiveness, High Cooperativeness]
    end

    S2 --> S3
    S1 --> S3
    S4 --> S3
    S5 --> S3
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Hierarchy of Conflict Redressal Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1 Direct Negotiation Between Parties] --> L2[Level 2 Supervisor or Manager Intervention]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Grievance Committee Review]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Senior Management or HR Head]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 External Conciliation Officer]
    L5 --> L6[Level 6 Arbitration or Labour Court Adjudication]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Aid:** In a 14-mark question, drawing a hierarchy diagram earns you 2–3 easy marks and makes your answer visually distinctive from peers.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Negotiation Process Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 Preparation and Planning] --> P2[Phase 2 Opening and Ground Rules]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Bargaining and Concession Exchange]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Closure and Agreement]
    P4 --> P5[Post Negotiation Implementation and Monitoring]
```
